segments.s161-g25: "بدو گفت موبد چه باید سپاه" is arranged in poetical word order: 'بدو' = 'to him/unto him' (prepositional phrase) comes first, then 'گفت' (past of 'say') and the subject 'موبد' (the mōbed/priest). The clause ends with the question-like sequence 'چه باید سپاه' = literally 'what must the army (do)?' where 'باید' is the modal 'must/ought' and 'چه' asks 'what'.
segments.s161-g26: In "چو خود رفت باید به آوردگاه" the subordinating word 'چو' means 'when'; 'خود' = 'himself/itself'; 'رفت' is the past 'went'. The modal 'باید' applies back to 'سپاه' (the army) mentioned earlier: 'when he himself went, (the army) must [go]'; 'به' = 'to' and 'آوردگاه' = 'battlefield'.
segments.s161-g27: 'چرا' = 'why'. 'خواسته' here functions as 'that which was asked / the requested thing' (a noun/nominal). 'داد' is the past 'gave' (he gave). 'باید' is the modal 'must/should', and 'بباد' is a poetic/passive-form meaning 'be blown away' or 'be lost to the wind' (expressing that something is wasted or scattered). The clause asks rhetorically why the requested thing has to be cast away.
segments.s161-g28: The phrase 'در گنج چندین چه باید گشاد' literally: 'in many a treasure (گنج چندین) what (چه) ought (باید) to be opened (گشاد)'. 'در' = 'in', 'گنج' = 'treasure', 'چندین' = 'several/many a', and 'گشاد' functions as 'opened' or 'to be opened' (adjectival/past participle sense). The speaker wonders what should be unsealed from the many treasures.
segments.s161-g29: 'دو بار' = 'twice' (two times). 'این سر نامور' = 'this renowned/famous head' (سر = head/life, نامور = renowned, postpositive adjective). 'گاه خویش' = 'its own/appointed time' — 'گاه' = 'time/occasion' and the enclitic 'ش' on 'خوی' marks possession ('its/his'); together 'at its time'. The sequence describes when the head was given over.
segments.s161-g30: 'سپردی' is the second-person past 'you entrusted/handed over'. The preposition 'به' = 'to'. 'تیزی' here means 'swiftness' or rapid force (not merely 'sharpness'). Another 'به' introduces the beneficiary/recipient phrase, and 'بدخواه خویش' = 'your ill-wisher/foe' (بدخواه = ill-wisher; خویش + ش = 'your/one's own' here marking possession 'your foe'). The whole clause says the addressee has twice consigned the famed head to the swift action of his enemy.
segments.s161-g31: 'کنون' = 'now'. 'پهلوانی' = 'heroic deed/heroism' (here an exhortation to act like a hero). The verbal imperative 'کن' (= do) attaches after the noun in the idiomatic command 'پهلوانی کن' = 'act heroically'. 'نگه کن' is another imperative 'look/watch' (here paired with 'گزین' to command attention and selection). 'گزین' is an imperative 'choose'. The imperatives are directed to the addressee: now perform heroism, watch, and choose.
segments.s161-g32: The adjectival 'سزاوار' = 'worthy/fit' is repeated for parallelism. 'جنگ' = 'war/battle' and 'کین' = 'vengeance/hatred'. Together the phrase specifies the qualities desired in the chosen person: deserving of battle and deserving of revenge.
segments.s161-g1: Compound nominal phrase: 'به مهر اندرون بود' places the king inside/within an inner space. 'به' is a prepositional/locative marker here, 'مهر' is used as a noun (literally 'mirth/affection' in older usage, part of a locative phrase), 'اندرون' = 'within/inner', and 'بود' is the past copula 'was'; the noun phrase 'شاه جهان' is the subject 'the king of the world'. Word order is verb-final for the copula construction ('... بود شاه جهان' = 'the king was ...').
segments.s161-g2: 'که' introduces a subordinate/clausal continuation ('that/when/who'); 'بشنید' is simple past 3rd-singular 'he heard'; 'گفتار' = 'speech/utterance' is the direct object; 'کارآگهان' (an archaic/poetic form of کارآگه) denotes the informant/one bringing tidings. The whole clause specifies what happened when the king was inside.
segments.s161-g3: 'که' again introduces the reported fact ('that'); 'افراسیاب' is the proper name Afrasiab; 'آمد' is the past tense 'came'. The conjunction 'و' links this clause to the following numerical specification ('and ...'). This clause reports the arrival.
segments.s161-g4: Nominal group describing the force: 'گزیده ز ترکان' = 'chosen from the Turks' (ز = 'from' in the poetic register), with 'شمرده سوار' = 'numbered/selected horsemen'—'شمرده' is a modifier 'numbered/selected' and 'سوار' is the head noun 'riders/horsemen'. The modifiers precede the noun in this poetic word order.
segments.s161-g5: 'سوی' = 'toward', followed by the place 'شهر ایران' = 'the city of Iran'. 'نهادست' is a fused/poetic perfective of نهادن + است meaning 'set/placed' or here 'set (his) face' = 'turned (his) face'; 'روی' = 'face' is the object of that action. Word order places the locative and place before the verb phrase.
segments.s161-g6: 'وزو' (poetic contraction = 'وز او' / 'from that/there') begins the result clause; 'گشت' is intransitive 'became/turned into'; 'کشور' = 'country/realm' is the subject; 'پر از' = 'full of'; and 'گفت و گوی' = 'talk and sayings' (i.e. conversation/gossip). The structure is 'from that, the realm became full of talk.'
segments.s161-g7: 'دل شاه کاووس' = 'the heart of King Kavus' (possessive/appositive ordering). 'ازان' = 'by/from that' (referring back to the prior news), and 'تنگ شد' is an idiomatic causative/inchoative: 'became narrow' → 'was grieved/was distressed/was constrained'. The copular 'شد' marks the change of state.
segments.s161-g8: 'که' introduces the consequence/explanation; 'از بزم رایش' = 'from the revels/banquets of his court' (بزم = feast; رایش here = 'his court/counsel/seat'); 'سوی جنگ شد' = 'he turned toward war' where 'سوی' = 'toward' and 'شد' = 'became/turned'. The clause expresses movement away from courtly pleasures toward warfare.
segments.s161-g161: که introduces a nominal/relative clause here meaning 'that' or simply 'that/there came'; آمد is simple past 3rd-person singular 'came'. The two coordinated nouns سپاهی و شاهی جوان follow the verb (a common poetic order), giving 'an army (سپاهی) and a young royal/king (شاهی جوان) arrived.'
segments.s161-g162: از ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' placed before the identifying phrase; گو here functions as the classical verbal particle 'said/called' (literally from 'say') linking the name/epithet that follows, so گو پیلتن پهلوان identifies the arriving man as the Piltan champion. Note the epithet/name follows the particle in this poetic word order.
segments.s161-g163: هیونی is the subject noun 'the beast/monster' (an animate/monstrous creature). به نزدیک is a directional phrase 'toward/near' that takes the proper name افراسیاب as its goal; word order places the direction after the noun for emphasis in poetry.
segments.s161-g164: برافگند is a past-tense verb 'he threw (off)' or 'he hurled'. It is followed by another verb برسان (literally 'bring/make reach'), here functioning as a causative/sequential verb 'caused/put' with کشتی as its object; برآب is an adverbial phrase 'onto the water.' The sequence reads as two actions: he hurled (something) and (he) put/sent the ship on the water.
segments.s161-g165: که آمد again introduces a reporting/relative clause 'that came'; ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from' before ایران. سپاهی گران is a noun plus adjective: سپاهی 'army/host' and گران literally 'heavy' used here metaphorically 'mighty/powerful' (adjective follows the noun).
segments.s161-g166: سپهبد سیاووش is a titled name in apposition (the military title سپهبد followed by the personal name سیاووش). و با او سران coordinates the addition 'and with him (با او) the leaders/chiefs (سران)'; با + pronoun introduces the companions.
segments.s161-g167: سپه کش is a compound: سپه 'army/host' plus the agentive element کش (here indexical '‑kesh/‑کش', meaning 'one who pulls/warrior of') yielding 'army‑fighter' or 'host‑bearer.' چو expresses a simile 'like/as' and introduces رستم ('Rostam'). گو again functions as the classical particle 'called/so‑called' linking the following name/place پیلتن; the phrase identifies the man as 'Piltan.'
segments.s161-g168: به یک دست خنجر به دیگر کفن is a paired adverbial formula: به + numeral + noun + noun gives 'with a dagger in one hand' (به یک دست خنجر), then the parallel به دیگر کفن 'and in the other (hand) a shroud.' The preposition به marks the locative/instrumental sense 'in/with,' and the omitted noun (hand) in the second half is recovered from the first.
segments.s161-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. گردآورد is a 3rd‑person perfective/present literary verb meaning 'gather/assemble'. مردم is the noun 'people' and کینه جوی is a compound where جوی (lit. 'seeking') modifies کینه to form the adjective 'vengeance‑seeking' or 'vengeful'; together they mean 'the vengeful people'. The word order is typical poetic Persian: temporal clause marker + verb + subject phrase.
segments.s161-g18: بتابد is a literary 3rd‑person singular present/optative from تابیدن (here sense 'turn/bend'), used narratively as 'turns/averts'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. پیمان و سوگند are two coordinated nouns 'covenant and oath'. روی is 'face' and here governed by بتابد: 'his face turns away from ...'. The clause means 'his face will turn away from covenant and oath.'
segments.s161-g19: جز از من is a prepositional phrase 'except from/by me' (only I). نشاید is a fixed literary impersonal expression 'it is not fitting/it should not be' (here implying prohibition for others). ورا is an enclitic prepositional pronoun 'toward him'. کینه is 'hatred/grudge' and خواه is the present stem of خواستن used here as 'to desire/wish'. Together the clause reads 'It should not be (the case), except from me, that anyone wishes hatred toward him' i.e. 'Only I may wish him ill.'
segments.s161-g20: کنم is 1st‑person singular present/optative of کردن 'I will do/make'. روز روشن is the noun phrase 'bright day' (روز modified by روشن). بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him'. بر is the preposition 'upon' and سیاه is the adjective 'black'. Word order here places the object phrase before the beneficiary phrase: 'I will make the bright day black upon him.'
segments.s161-g21: مگر here functions as 'unless/except'. گم (lit. 'lost') with کنم ('I make') forms a causative sense 'I make (it) lost' = 'I blot out/erase'. نام او is 'his name' (here best understood as 'his name/reputation'). در جهان is the locative 'in the world'. The clause therefore means 'Unless I erase/blot out his name in the world.'
segments.s161-g22: وگر نه is a conditional 'otherwise / but if not'. چو is the simile marker 'like/as'. تیر از کمان is a straightforward image 'an arrow from the bow'. ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly'. This short clause is elliptical—no finite verb appears; the simile (‘he will be struck/fall’) is implied from the context: 'otherwise (he will fall) suddenly like an arrow from the bow.'
segments.s161-g23: سپه سازد is a verbal phrase where سازد (3rd‑person singular of ساختن in the sense 'raise/assemble') takes سپه 'army' as its object: 'he raises an army'. و connects the clauses. رزم ایران کند literally 'makes war of Iran' = 'wages war upon Iran' where کند is the general verb 'does/makes'. The order reflects poetic inversion but the meaning is 'he raises an army and wages war on Iran.'
segments.s161-g24: بسی is an intensifier 'many/much' (here 'in many places' or 'greatly'). زین literally 'saddle/trappings' is used metonymically for mounted force or places associated with war; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. بوم is 'homeland, native soil'. ویران is the adjective/participle 'ruined/desolate' and کند is the verb 'makes/does'. Together the phrase means 'he will, in numerous places, lay waste the land and homesteads' (i.e. desolate country and dwellings).
segments.s161-g89: ز is the poetic preposition here meaning 'with' or 'by', followed by a coordinated list of nouns joined by و ('and'). The nouns are simply enumerated objects (شمشیر, گرز, کلاه, کمر) governed by ز; کمر here means 'girdle/belt'.
segments.s161-g90: همان خود is an emphatic continuation: همان 'the same/that' + خود as intensifier = 'the very/own (ones)'. It continues the list with درع (armor), سنان (spear) and سپر (shield); the conjunction و again links the items.
segments.s161-g91: به + indefinite noun (گنجی) marks direction/recipient ('to/for a treasure'), and the relative clause introduced by که ('which') modifies گنجی. نابرید is a negative participle (نا + برید 'cut') meaning 'uncut' or 'intact', applied to جامه ('cover/garment'), so the clause describes a treasure whose covering was uncut.
segments.s161-g92: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'. نزد followed by a person marks the recipient ('to / in the presence of Siavash'), and کلید is the direct object placed after the oblique phrase — idiomatic Persian word order here is verb + oblique phrase + object: 'sent (it) to Siavash the key.'
segments.s161-g93: که introduces reported instruction/purpose ('that' / 'so that'). The repeated preposition بر ('for/on') applies to two nouns: جان ('life') and خواسته ('wish/desire'); کدخدای is the possessor 'of the kâdkhudâ (village-head)', so the phrase means 'for the life and for the wish of the village-head.'
segments.s161-g94: توی ('in, among') + ساز (here a noun meaning 'device/plan/means') + کن (imperative 'make/do') gives the command 'devise/arrange among yourselves'. تا marks purpose 'so that', and چه آیدت رای is a clause 'what counsel/plan comes to you' (آید with enclitic -ت 'to you', and رای = 'counsel/plan').
segments.s161-g95: گزین کرد is the past tense 'he chose/selected'. ازان is an archaic form 'from those', so 'he chose from those renowned men'; نامداران modifies the men as 'renowned', and سوار specifies them as 'horsemen/mounted'. The verb precedes its oblique object phrase, a common classical word order.
segments.s161-g96: دلیران جنگی is a compound: دلیران 'the brave ones' modified by جنگی 'warlike/fighter', i.e. 'brave warlike fighters'. ده و دو هزار is a poetic numeral expression meaning 'ten and two thousand' = 12,000; Persian frequently lists numerals in this additive way.
segments.s161-g105: ز + NP: the preposition ز (here 'from') introduces a list of groups. گردان جنگی is a noun phrase with the modifier جنگی ('warlike/military') after گردان ('troop/regiment'); نام‌آوران is a coordinated noun meaning 'the renowned ones' and continues the same prepositional phrase. In this poetic line the whole phrase names people coming from a source.
segments.s161-g106: چو ... و چون ...: the particle چو means 'as/like' and sets up a simile or examples. بهرام is a proper name ('Bahram'); the second چون repeats the simile before زنگه شاوران. زنگه here behaves like a proper name (archaic/poetic) followed by شاوران ('advisers'), so the phrase means 'like Zangah the advisers'. The clause is elliptical (no finite verb) and appositive to the previous phrase.
segments.s161-g107: همان پنج موبد از ایرانیان is a full nominal phrase: همان 'those/same' + numeral + noun (پنج موبد 'five mōbeds/priests'), followed by the partitive/prepositional از ایرانیان 'from (among) the Iranians' specifying origin. This entire phrase functions as the subject of the verb that follows.
segments.s161-g108: برافراختند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb 'they raised/erected' (بر + افراشتن, used here in the simple past). اختر کاویان is the object: اختر 'star' qualified by کاویان 'of the Kayanian/kings'. The object follows the verb here for emphatic/poetic order but is governed by برافراختند as what was raised.
segments.s161-g109: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of result/purpose here ('that' / 'so that'), followed by جمله ('all/everyone') as the subject of the subordinate clause and بیرون شدند 'became outside / went out' as the verbal predicate (شدند = past plural of شدن used in the resultive sense).
segments.s161-g110: A chain of adverbial/prepositional phrases describing motion: ز پهلو 'from the flank' (ز + noun), سوی 'toward' introduces the destination, and دشت و هامون are the coordinated destinations ('plain and hamun/desert'). The final شدند repeats the past plural verb 'they went/became' agreeing with the subject in the previous clause.
segments.s161-g111: تو گفتی که: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you' and گفتی is the past 2nd-singular verb 'you said', introducing a subordinate content clause with که. اندر زمین جای نیست is that subordinate clause: اندر زمین 'in/on the earth', جای 'place', and نیست 'is not/there is none'. Together it reports what 'you said.'
segments.s161-g112: Another explanatory subordinate clause introduced by که: بر خاک او 'on/over its soil' (بر + خاک + possessive او), نعل را is a definite/specific object marked by را ('the horseshoe'), and پای نیست literally 'there is no foot' — idiomatically 'there is no hoof/foot to take a horseshoe.' The construction states a lack/existence (نیست) of what would support a horseshoe.
segments.s161-g41: به دل گفت = a reflexive phrasing meaning “he said to his heart” (i.e. he told himself). Word order is flexible in poetry: the preposition به + object (دل) precedes the verb گفت. من سازم این رزمگاه is direct speech reported immediately: من (subject) + سازم (present/subjunctive with ب‑ dropped for poetic meter, here used as a volitional/future-like ‘I will make/prepare’) + این رزمگاه (direct object).
segments.s161-g42: به خوبی بگویم بخواهم ز شاه: به خوبی (adverbial phrase) = “well/properly” (به here functions adverbially, ‘‑ly’); بگویم and بخواهم are present-subjunctive/volitional forms (بـ‑ + present stem) used to express intended actions (‘I will say / I will ask’). ز means ‘from / of’ and introduces the object of خواستن: ز شاه = ‘from the king’ (i.e. request/appeal to the king).
segments.s161-g43: مگر here functions as a rhetorical interrogative (“could it be that…?”). کم رهایی is the object phrase (“a little freedom/relief”), with کم modifying رهایی. دهد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive/potential ‘gives/would give’. دادگر follows the verb as the subject (‘the just one’ = the just ruler/judge); poetic word order (verb before subject) is common.
segments.s161-g44: ز = ‘because of / on account of’ (preposition). سودابه is the proper name ‘Sudabeh’. و is the conjunction ‘and’. گفت و گوی is the conventional compound meaning ‘the talk / the saying / conversation’ (گفت and گوی here form the noun phrase ‘speech/words’). پدر is the possessor (‘of the father’); the genitive relation is implicit (poetic style omits an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s161-g45: دگر‌گر here serves as an archaic/poetic conditional/connective (‘if moreover / if otherwise / should’). ازین = از این ‘from this’ (written in split form for meter). کار = ‘deed/act’. نام آورم is a compound where نام (fame) plus آورم (‘I bring’) means ‘I will bring/name I will win’ = ‘I will gain fame’; آورم is present/subjunctive/volitional expressing intention or hypothetical result.
segments.s161-g46: چنین = ‘such/like this’ (modifying لشکری). لشکری را = the army (را marks the definite/direct object). به دام آورم = a verbal phrase ‘I will bring into a trap / I will trap’; به دام is the prepositional object ‘into a snare’, and آورم is the same volitional/present-subjunctive verb ‘I will bring’ used here as ‘I will cause/entrap’. Word order places the object (لشکری را) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s161-g47: بشد here is an archaic/poetic verbal form from شدن meaning roughly ‘he did/it became’ used narratively (here ‘he girded himself / he prepared’). با کمر = ‘with his girdle/waist’ (با + کمر); پیش = ‘before / in the presence of’. کاووس شاه places the proper name before the title (Kavus, the king) in apposition, a normal poetic pattern.
segments.s161-g48: بدو گفت = به + او + گفت ‘he said to him’ (بدو is the contracted/directional form ‘to him’). The quotation that follows is reported: من دارم این پایگاه = ‘I have / hold this post’ where دارم is present tense ‘I have/hold’ and این پایگاه names the position/stronghold. The clause is a straightforward declarative in direct speech.
segments.s161-g145: چو = 'when', a temporal conjunction introducing the clause. یک ماه بگذشت is a time phrase: ماه (month) is the period noun and بگذشت is the past verb 'passed' (time passed). لشکر براند follows as the main clause: لشکر (army) is the subject and براند is a classical narrative verb meaning 'moved/drave on' (here ‘moved off’); the verb is placed after the subject, typical of Persian word order.
segments.s161-g146: گوپیلتن رفت و دستان بماند is two coordinated past clauses joined by و. رفت is the simple past 'went/left' with گوپیلتن as subject. دستان is treated here as a (proper) name and precedes the verb بماند 'remained' (past sense 'was left' / 'remained'); the order Name + verb is standard, and the contrast is implied (one person leaves, the other stays).
segments.s161-g147: سپاهی برفتند با پهلوان: سپاهی is a collective noun 'troops/soldiers' serving as subject. برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/went'; با introduces accompaniment and is followed by پهلوان 'the champion/hero'. Word order is subject + verb + prepositional accompaniment.
segments.s161-g148: ز زابل هم از کابل و هندوان lists origins. ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from'; زابل is the place name. هم 'also' connects sources; the second origin is expressed with از کابل و هندوان 'from Kabul and the Indians'. The repetition of the preposition (ز ... از ...) is a poetic/archaic coordination.
segments.s161-g149: ز هر سو که ... لشکری is a relative/conditional phrase: ز هر سو 'from every direction' (ز = from, هر سو = every side). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/where'. بد نامور is a compound adjective: بد (bad/ill) + نامور (famed) = 'ill‑famed/notorious'. لشکری ('an army') is the noun modified by the clause.
segments.s161-g150: بخواند و بیامد are two verbs describing what that army did: بخواند (from خواندن) here means 'called/summoned' (i.e., 'mustered/raised') and is used in the narrative past sense; و links the actions; بیامد is the past 'came'. به شهر هری is the destination phrase: به + شهر + هری = 'to the city of Hari'.
segments.s161-g151: ازیشان فراوان پیاده ببرد: ازیشان is the contracted form of از ایشان 'from them'. فراوان modifies پیاده and means 'many/plentiful' — here پیاده functions as a noun 'infantry/foot-soldiers'. ببرد (برد) is the past action 'he took/carried off' (third person singular); the verb comes at the end of the clause.
segments.s161-g152: بنه زنگه شاوران را سپرد: this clause ends with the past verb سپرد 'he entrusted/handed over'. شاوران را is the definite object (شاوران 'advisers', with the object marker را). بنه زنگه precedes the object and appears to be a compound/name phrase (likely a camp/group or proper name) that is being handed over; several of these words function as proper names here, so the exact lexical senses listed above do not match and are treated as names in context.
segments.s161-g113: سراندر is a poetic prepositional adverb meaning 'within' or 'in', placed before the noun phrase; سپهر اخْتر کاویان is the noun phrase 'the star of the Kayanians' (اختر 'star' modified by کاویان 'Kayanian'). Word order is elevated/poetic: the locative 'سراندر سپهر' ('within the sky') precedes the subject.
segments.s161-g114: چو is a simile marker 'like/as'; ماه درخشنده is a noun + adjective 'the shining moon' (درخشنده modifying ماه); اندر میان is a locative phrase 'in the middle' (اندر = 'in/within', میان = 'middle/among'), completing the image of where and how the star appears.
segments.s161-g115: ز پهلو 'from the flank/side' uses the preposition ز 'from' + noun پهلو 'side, flank'. برون‌رفت here functions as a verbal expression 'came forth/stepped out' (poetic compound), and the agent 'کاووس شاه' follows it in the inverted, elevated word order (verb before subject) typical of epic style.
segments.s161-g116: یکی functions here as 'one / someone / at once' (an immediate action marker in context), تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift', and برگشت is the verb 'returned/turned back'. گرد سپاه is a postverbal prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/about the army' (گرد = 'around/toward', سپاه = 'army'), so the clause means 'someone/one quickly turned back toward the army.'
segments.s161-g117: یکی here means 'someone' (an unspecified person). آفرین کرد is a light verb construction where آفرین (an acclamation/praise) is the noun and کرد 'made/uttered' turns it into 'uttered a praise/exclamation'. پرمایه qualifies آفرین as 'substantial/abundant' and کی in this context is a vocative/name-element referring to the Kay (an honorific/name element); the phrase as a whole means 'someone made a hearty acclamation to the Kay.'
segments.s161-g118: که‌ای here introduces a vocative/relative exclamation 'O you who...', with که functioning as a relative/introducer and ای the vocative particle. نامداران 'the renowned/illustrious' and فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' are epithets describing the addressees; پی (here) means 'following' or 'in the train/of those who follow' and completes the laudatory phrase addressing the famed company.
segments.s161-g119: مبادا is a negative optative 'may it not be / lest'. جز از بخت means 'except by/through fortune' (جز = 'except', از = 'from/by', بخت = 'fortune/luck'), and همراهتان 'that accompanies you' qualifies whose fortune it is; the phrase sets up a wish/hope that nothing adverse happen except by (luck/fate) that is with them.
segments.s161-g120: شده is the perfect/passive participle 'has become' (or a verbal adjective 'become'), تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous', and دیدار بدخواهتان 'the meeting of/with your ill-wishers' is the noun phrase (دیدار = 'meeting', بدخواهتان = 'your ill-wishers'). Together they express 'the meeting with your enemies/ill-wishers become dark/ominous'.
segments.s161-g65: بدو گفت is an inverted ditransitive-like sequence: بدو ('to him', a dative/locative clitic phrase) precedes گفت (the past tense verb 'said'), so literally 'to him he said'. همزور تو پیل نیست is a verbless predication where همزور ('equal in strength') predicates تو ('you') and is compared to پیل ('an elephant'); نیست negates the predicate—overall: 'You are not equal in strength to an elephant' / 'You are no match for an elephant.'
segments.s161-g66: چو introduces a simile ('as' / 'like'). گرد پی رخش is a noun phrase 'the dust (گرد) following/after (پی) his steed (رخش)' with پی functioning as a postpositional/relational word 'after, following'. The clause closes with تو نیل نیست, where تو is the subject and نیل نیست is a nominal predicate 'are not the Nile' (نیست negates the identification); together: 'as the dust after his steed, you are not the Nile'—a poetic way to demean the addressee by comparing him unfavourably.
segments.s161-g67: ز گیتی ('of/from the world'—ز is a poetic genitive/partitive) sets the domain; هنرمند و خامش توی is a nominal description placed after the domain: هنرمند ('skillful person') and خامش ('silent') modify an implied person. توی here works as a locative/relational particle ('among/in') linking the attribute to the world: 'among the world (people), skillful and silent are you.' The word order is poetic (domain first, predicate after).
segments.s161-g68: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause referring back to the preceding characterization ('for/that'), and پروردگار سیاوش is the predicate noun phrase 'the nurturer/lord of Siavash'. The final توی repeats the locative/relational particle (in classical usage it helps attach the predicate to the addressed person), so the clause says essentially 'for you are the lord/nurturer of Siavash'—an appositive relative explaining why he is counted among the skilled and silent.
segments.s161-g69: چو marks a temporal/conditional clause 'when/just as'. آهن ببندد is a present/future-or-general clause with ببندد (subjunctive/present form 'binds/are bound'); به کان در combines به + کان + در as a locative phrase 'into/in the mine' (کان = mine/ore, در = in), and گهر is the logical object 'a gem'. The clause reads poetically 'when iron is bound within the mine (کان در), (a) gem (گهر) ...' linking enclosure and hidden value.
segments.s161-g70: گشاده شود is a passive/impersonal result 'is opened / becomes revealed' (شود = 'become' as auxiliary-like verb). چون begins the comparison 'as/so', and تو بستی کمر is a protasis where تو is the subject, بستی is the past tense 'you bound/girded', and کمر (here) is 'girdle/waist'—so 'as when you gird your waist' (i.e. when you prepare yourself), something is revealed. The simile parallels hidden treasure revealed by action with the addressee's own revealed excellence when he girds himself.
segments.s161-g71: This clause is sequence-of-events word order: سیاوش بیامد ('Siavash came') is the main event; کمر بر میان is a compact prepositional/figural phrase where کمر ('waist/girdle') plus بر میان ('onto/into the midst') describes his posture or placement—in classical Persian idiom کمر بستن (to bind the waist) means 'to gird oneself' (prepare), so here it conveys 'having girded his waist he came into the middle/in front'.
segments.s161-g72: سخن گفت با من is literal 'he spoke words with me' (سخن = speech/words, گفت as past-tense 'spoke'—note the lexical senses list lacks a verbal sense so verb sense is marked specially). چو introduces the simile 'like/as', and شیر ژیان is an image: شیر ('lion') qualified by ژیان ('living'/'full of life' in this context) meaning 'a living/ferocious lion'—so the whole: 'he spoke with me like a lion (i.e., boldly/fiercely).' Word order places the simile after the speech for emphasis.
segments.s161-g201: همی is an adverbial/intensifying particle 'ever/still' placed before the clause. آفرین here is a noun/exclamation 'praise' or 'acclamation', and باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)'; in classical Persian the optative باد commonly follows the thing wished for (آفرین باد = 'may there be praise'). بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'upon the king' marking the beneficiary/target of the wish.
segments.s161-g202: همه here functions adverbially 'altogether/wholly' modifying the following phrase. نیکوی is the adjective 'good/excellent' placed before the noun (classical Persian often puts adjectives before the noun). باد again is the optative particle 'may', and فرجام کار is a noun phrase 'end/outcome of the affair' (two nouns juxtaposed where modern Persian would use an ezāfe or possessive relation).
segments.s161-g203: به بلخ is a prepositional phrase 'to/into Balkh' placed before the verb for emphasis. آمدم is the 1st‑person past 'I came'. شاد و پیروز are predicative adjectives 'happy and victorious' that follow the verb, and بخت ('fortune') follows پیروز as a modifying noun forming the phrase 'پیروز بخت' (victorious by fortune).
segments.s161-g204: به here is used instrumentally/adverbially 'by/through' (classical 'by the glory of...'). فر is the abstract noun 'royal glory' placed before جهاندار ('world‑holder/sovereign')—classical Persian often omits the ezāfe. باتاج is a fossilized/prepositional compound meaning 'with crown' (با + تاج fused), و links 'crown' and تخت ('throne'), which is the final noun of the coordinate phrase.
segments.s161-g205: سه روز is a duration 'three days' placed before the clause. اندرین جنگ is a locative/temporal phrase 'in this war' (اندرین = 'in these/this'), and شد is the past 'became/happened' used here with روزگار ('time') to mean 'the time was [spent]/the days passed'—classical word order places the duration and locative before the verb.
segments.s161-g206: چهارم is an adverbial numeral 'on the fourth (day)'. ببخشود is a classical 3rd‑person past form; in this context it means 'granted/bestowed' (classical Persian verbs often retain a prefixed b-). پروردگار follows the verb as the agent 'the Lord/God'—poetry frequently places the subject after the verb for rhythm.
segments.s161-g207: سپهرم here functions as a proper name (sense 'personal name') rather than literally 'my sky'. به ترمذ شد uses به + place + شد to mean 'went/arrived to Termez' (شد used intransitively). و links the next noun بارمان (Barman), which begins the following coordinated clause.
segments.s161-g208: به کردار here means 'in the manner of / like' (an adverbial phrase). ناوک is 'arrow/arrowhead' and کردار ناوک = 'in the fashion of an arrowhead'; بجست is a classical past 'leapt/sprung' (the b‑prefixed past), and از کمان 'from the bow' is the source phrase following the verb. The clause therefore describes a rapid, arrow‑like motion.
segments.s161-g33: چنین داد پاسخ بدیشان که من is literally 'thus gave an answer to them that I...'. داد is a past-tense, third-person singular verb ('gave/said'); پاسخ is its direct object ('answer'). بدیشان ('to them') marks the indirect object. که introduces reported speech/content of the answer (a subordinate clause), and من is the subject of that subordinate clause placed before the clause-final verb (typical older/poetic word order).
segments.s161-g34: نبینم کسی را بدین انجمن contains the content clause introduced by که. نبینم is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن + بینم) used here as a simple negative statement 'I do not see'. کسی in negative contexts means 'anyone'. را is the definite/object marker attached to کسی. بدین is built from ب- + این and means 'in/at this'; انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting' and is the place indicated by بدین.
segments.s161-g35: که دارد پی و تاب افراسیاب: که functions as a causal 'for/because'. دارد is a present-tense third-person singular 'has/possesses'. پی here means 'pursuit/following' (i.e. being pursued) and تاب denotes force/endurance/power; together they describe Afrasiab's active pursuit/strength. افراسیاب is the name (possessor).
segments.s161-g36: مرا رفت باید چو کشتی بر آب: this is an older word order where مرا (me) is split in the text; the clause means 'I must go'. باید is the modal 'must/ought', رفت is the verb 'go/leave' (past-stem used in a present sense here), and چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. کشتی بر آب 'a ship on the water' completes the simile. Note: the initial 'م' in the word list is actually part of مرا (the object/experiencer) and not the copula shown in the lemma list.
segments.s161-g37: شما بازگردید تا من کنون: بازگردید is a second-person plural imperative or jussive 'return/turn back' directed at شما ('you, plural'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that/in order that') whose subject is من and which will contain the subjunctive verb. کنون is an adverb 'now', placed after the subject for emphasis.
segments.s161-g38: بپیچم یکی دل برین رهنمون: بپیچم is a present/subjunctive first-person singular 'I turn / I will turn' expressing the speaker's intended action (here following تا). یکی دل is the direct object 'one heart' (one = numeric quantifier before the noun). بر + این (written as برین) marks direction/target 'to this', so برین رهنمون = 'to this guidance/guide'.
segments.s161-g39: سیاوش ازان دل پراندیشه کرد: ازان ('from that' / 'because of that') gives the reason. پراندیشه is an adjectival/compound form meaning 'full of thought' or 'pensive'; the construction 'دل پراندیشه کرد' means 'made his heart full of thought' or 'became pensive', with کرد as the causative/performative verb 'made/did'.
segments.s161-g40: روان را از اندیشه چون بیشه کرد: روان را is the definite object 'the soul/spirit'. از اندیشه here means 'out of/through thought' (indicating cause or medium). چون introduces a simile 'like/as' and بیشه is 'a grove/thicket'. کرد again is 'made' — the whole line says he made his soul, by/with thought, like a grove (i.e. full or overgrown with thoughts). Word order places the object before the adverbial phrase and simile, with the verb at the end (typical poetic order).
segments.s161-g81: سیاوش is the fronted subject (proper name). پناه و روان are two coordinated predicative nouns ('refuge and spirit/soul') describing him. منست is a poetic contraction corresponding to من است (‘is mine’/‘is my’), acting as the copula that links the nouns to the speaker (i.e. ‘is my …’); classical Persian often places the predicate before or after such copula without an overt ezafe.
segments.s161-g82: سر تاج او is a genitive phrase 'the top/summit of his crown' (note the possessive pronoun او follows the possessed noun, as is normal in older Persian). آسمان منست is the predicate: آسمان ('sky/heaven') with the same poetic copula منست ('is mine' / 'is my ...'), so literally 'the summit of his crown is my sky.' Word order is somewhat topical (possessed phrase first, predicate after).
segments.s161-g83: چو here = 'when' and بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (3rd sing.). ازو = 'from/of him/it' points to what was heard. آفرین کرد is an idiomatic past-tense phrase 'he uttered praise' (literally 'made an afrin'); و گفت = 'and he said.' This strings the reaction (exclamation) and ensuing speech together.
segments.s161-g84: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that he said:'). با جان پاکت = 'with/alongside your pure soul' (جان = 'life/soul', پاکت = پاک + enclitic -ت 'your purity/your pure'). خرد باد جفت is an optative formula: باد is the optative particle 'may it be', خرد = 'wisdom', and جفت = 'paired/mate' — together 'may wisdom be paired with your pure soul.' Word order places the accompaniment phrase (با جان پاکت) before the optative clause.
segments.s161-g85: وزان پس = 'from that time/thereupon, after that' (وز + آن + پس contracted/poetic ordering). خروشیدن is a verbal noun/masdar 'the roaring/sounding', governing the instruments نای و کوس ('ney and drum') — together 'the roaring/sounding of the ney and drum.' Classical Persian often uses the masdar + instrument sequence without an explicit ezafe.
segments.s161-g86: برآمد = 'arose/issued forth' (past), applied to the preceding sound; بیامد = 'came' (past) introduces the following action. سپهدار طوس is the title + name 'the commander Tus' (title before name). The two past verbs give successive events: the sound rose, and then the commander came forward.
segments.s161-g87: به درگاه = 'at/to the royal court/threshold' (prepositional phrase marking location). بر انجمن شد سپاه is an instance of classical inversion: بر انجمن شد = 'took position at/appeared before the assembly' and سپاه (the army) appears after the verb as the syntactic subject. شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was' used to mark the event.
segments.s161-g88: در گنج دینار = 'in/at the treasury of dinars' (گنج دینار = the treasury or chest of coins). بگشاد شاه is another inversion: the verb بگشاد (poetic past of گشود, 'opened') precedes the subject شاه ('the king'), so 'the king opened the treasure of dinars.'
segments.s161-g233: "ازان پس" (from/after that) begins a temporal clause; "که" introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'; "پیروز گشتی" is an archaic past second-person form meaning 'you became victorious' (the copula/verb is expressed by گشتی), and "به جنگ" is an adverbial phrase 'in battle' placed after the verb. Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: temporal + subordinator + verb phrase + adverbial.
segments.s161-g234: This clause gives the consequence/action. "به کار اندرون" = 'toward/for the inner (household) affairs' (به + noun phrase). "کرد" is the verbal element 'do/make' and is used here with the modal "باید" (must/ought) which follows; "درنگ" is a noun 'pause/delay' serving as the complement. In older Persian the modal "باید" can follow the verb phrase, so the literal order is roughly 'to the household affairs do — it is necessary — a pause' = 'you must pause before handling household affairs'.
segments.s161-g235: An impersonal prohibition formed with the modal "نباید" (one must not/should not) followed by the verbal-noun phrase "پراگنده کردن سپاه" = 'to scatter the army' (پراگنده + کردن + direct object سپاه). The infinitival/verbal-noun construction after نباید expresses the forbidden action.
segments.s161-g236: "بپیمای" is an archaic/poetic second-person imperative or hortative of پیمودن 'to traverse/march' (the prefixed ب- appears in older Persian imperatives). "روز" qualifies the march as 'by day'. "و برآرای گاه" is a second imperative: برآرای (imperative of برآرایدن/آراستن) = 'array/prepare' and گاه = 'the time/appointed occasion'. The two imperatives are coordinated: 'march by day and prepare at the time'.
segments.s161-g237: "که" introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'; "آن ترک" = 'that Turk' (subject). The two adjectives "بدپیشه و ریمنست" form the predicate, with the copula attached to the final adjective (ریمنست = 'is reymān/objectionable/ill-disposed' in context). In classical Persian adjectives often appear before the final copula which is suffixed once.
segments.s161-g238: A correlative descriptive clause introduced by "که" again. The pair "هم ... و هم ..." means 'both ... and ...' with the predicates "بدنژادست" (is ill-born/low-born) and "بدتنست" (is ill-bodied/of bad physique). Each predicate carries its copula (-ست) in this poetic register.
segments.s161-g239: "همان" (that very one) is the subject. The prepositional phrase "با کلاهست" uses کلاه with the copular suffix -ست to indicate 'he is with a cap / wearing a cap'; "و با دستگاه" adds another accompaniment 'and with equipment/gear' (با + دستگاه). The sequence lists the subject's outward trappings before the action.
segments.s161-g240: "همی" is an adverb marking a habitual or instantaneous action ('now/indeed' in poetry); "سر برآرد" is an older verb form meaning 'he raises/lifts his head' (برآرد = brings up/raises). The preposition "ز" (poetic form of از) marks comparison/source 'from' or 'like' here, followed by "تابنده ماه" = 'the shining moon' — a simile: he lifts his head like the radiant moon. Verb is third-person singular present/habitual.
segments.s161-g9: ۱) 'یکی' here is indefinite 'one / someone' (a certain person) and functions as the subject. 'انجمن کرد' is a past-tense verb phrase 'held an assembly / convened' (noun + کرد is common in classical Persian). 'از ایرانیان' is a prepositional phrase 'from among the Iranians' determining who did the convening. The order is slightly inverted in epic style: subject + verb + adjunct.
segments.s161-g10: 'کسی را' is the definite/object-marked noun phrase 'a certain man' (را marks him as the direct object of 'انجمن کرد' — i.e. he summoned/collected this man). 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who…'. 'بد نیکخواه کیان' is a two-word description: 'بد' (bad/ill) modifies 'نیکخواه' (well-wisher), so together 'an ill-wisher; one with ill will', and 'کیان' names the addressees of that ill will (the Kian kings). Adjective phrase follows the noun in this older style.
segments.s161-g11: 'بدیشان' = 'to them' (بدی-شان, dative with 3rd-pl enclitic). 'چنین' = 'thus/so' (manner adverb). 'گفت' is the past tense 'said' (the lexical sense 'said' is intended here). The proper name 'کافراسیاب' follows the verb in the epic inversion: 'to them thus said Kafrasiyab.'
segments.s161-g12: This is a poetic enumerative series using repeated preposition 'ز' ('from/by')—'ز باد و ز آتش ز خاک و ز آب' — listing the four elements. The repeated 'ز' is a stylistic device; each noun (باد, آتش, خاک, آب) is simply coordinated and receives the same prepositional relation.
segments.s161-g13: 'همانا' is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed.' 'که' introduces the clause. 'ایزد' = 'God' (or divine Providence) is the subject. 'نکردش' is past negative 'did not do/make' with the enclitic object 'ش' = 'him/it.' 'سرشت' here functions as 'nature' / 'the making' — the phrase means 'God did not give him that nature / God did not make him so.' Note the archaic/poetic word order (verb before noun complement).
segments.s161-g14: 'مگر' here is adversative 'but rather.' 'خود سپهرش' = 'the heavens themselves' (خود adds emphasis; سپهرش is 'سپهر' + possessive/enclitic but functions idiomatically as 'the sky/heavens themselves' as agent). 'دگرگونه' = 'differently/other-wise.' 'کشت' is used metaphorically as 'planted/formed/shaped' (not literal 'killing'): 'the heavens themselves fashioned him differently.'
segments.s161-g15: 'که' links this clause as purpose/result ('so that' / 'in that...'). 'چندین' = 'many / several' (adverbial quantifier). 'به سوگند' = 'by oath' (instrumental phrase), and 'پیمان کند' = 'make a covenant / exact a pledge' — 'کند' is the present/subjunctive form used in epic narration to express what he will do or may do (third-person singular).
segments.s161-g16: 'زبان را' is the direct object with the accusative marker 'را' ('the tongue' = speech). 'به خوبی' is an adverbial phrase 'by/with goodness / kindly.' 'گروگان کند' literally 'make a hostage' — an idiom meaning 'to hold someone's speech/tongue as a pledge' i.e. to silence or secure their compliance by fair words. Together this continues the action from the previous segment: he binds many by oath and, by kindness, holds their tongues hostage.
segments.s161-g121: This is a nominal‑infinitival wish/clause built with به + N + شدن: به نیک اختر و تندرستی شدن = 'to become/have a good/auspicious star and health'. شدن is the infinitive/verb meaning 'to become / to be granted' and sits at the end; نیک اختر ('auspicious star') and تندرستی ('health') are coordinated by و. The preposition به introduces the state being wished for.
segments.s161-g122: A parallel infinitival phrase introduced by به: به پیروزی و شاد باز آمدن = 'to come back in victory and happiness'. آمدن is the infinitive 'to come/return'; باز (here 'again'/'back') precedes آمدن to make 'return'; شاد modifies the manner ('joyful'). The phrase parallels the previous 'به ... شدن' wish.
segments.s161-g123: وزان جایگه is an adverbial phrase 'thereupon / from that place' (وزان) + 'place' (جایگه). The main clause follows: کوس بر پیل بست — verb آخر (بست) 'tied/fastened' with object order 'کوس' (drum) and locative 'بر پیل' ('onto the elephant'). This is ordinary past tense word order (object + adjunct + verb).
segments.s161-g124: به گردان بفرمود = 'he ordered/commanded the troop' where به + NP marks the indirect/object of the verb بفرمود (honorific past of 'say/order'). The coordination 'و خود برنشست' ties another action: خود 'himself' + برنشست (past) 'sat upon / mounted' — simple past, third person singular; بر‑ prefix indicates 'on/onto (a mount)'.
segments.s161-g125: دو دیده 'two eyes' is the head noun phrase; پر از آب is a post‑nominal adjectival phrase meaning 'full of water' (i.e. tear‑filled). کاووس شاه follows as the possessor/title (appositional identification): 'the two eyes of King Kavus.' Word order places the quantity and description before the proper name.
segments.s161-g126: همی بود is an imperfective/past habitual construction (همی marking continuous aspect) + بود 'was'. یک روز 'one day' is a temporal adverbial; با او به راه = 'on the road with him / travelling with him' where به راه means 'on the journey'. The clause as a whole: 'one day he was travelling with him.'
segments.s161-g127: سرانجام = 'finally / in the end'. مر is an archaic/pronounal form (here printed as 'مر') — in classical Persian forms like this can be emphatic or clitic; it appears before the object phrase. یکدگر را کنار = 'one another to the side/aside' with را marking the definite object and کنار indicating 'beside/aside'. Together this prepares the object for the verb گرفتند that follows.
segments.s161-g128: گرفتند is the past plural verb 'they took / they seized / they drew' (here 'took one another aside'). هر دو = 'both (of them)' emphasizing the plural subject; چو introduces a simile 'like', followed by ابر بهار 'a spring cloud' — a poetic comparison. Word order places the verb before the subject emphasis and the simile follows.
segments.s161-g137: سپه را سوی زابلستان کشید is a simple past transitive clause. سپه را is the definite direct object plus the object marker را (supply/contrastive object marking after the noun). سوی زابلستان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward Zabulistan’ placed before the verb. کشید is the past tense verb (third singular) meaning here ‘moved/led/marched’ (poetic use of کشیدن).
segments.s161-g138: ابا پیلتن سوی دستان کشید places a (proper) name phrase in subject position: ‘Aba Piltan’. سوی دستان is again the directional prepositional phrase. کشید is the past-tense verb as above. Note the straightforward Subject–(Adverbial)–Verb order; there is no را because the subject (Aba Piltan) is explicit.
segments.s161-g139: همی بود یکچند با رود و می is an adjectival/adverbial clause describing duration and accompaniment. همی before the verb gives a continuing or habitual sense; بود is the past copula ‘was/ remained’; یکچند = ‘for a while’; با + رود و می = ‘with river(s) and wine’ (an adverbial phrase of company or circumstance).
segments.s161-g140: به نزدیک دستان فرخنده پی is a locative/directional phrase. به نزدیک = ‘to/near’ (به + adjective-like نزدیک); دستان is the place/name headed by the following modifiers; فرخنده modifies the following word (either as adjective ‘auspicious/fortunate’ or as a name); پی here is a postposition or noun meaning ‘after/following’ or part of a name. Word order is prepositional (به نزدیک) + place + modifiers.
segments.s161-g141: گهی با تهمتن بدی می‌بدست opens with گهی ‘sometimes’. با تهمتن is the comitative/with phrase ‘with Tahmatan’. بدی here is an archaic/polysyllabic element (in the poem it functions as a predicate form—see note) and می‌بدست is a present/imperfective verb (mi‑ prefix) ‘obtains/receives’ or habitual ‘would get/receive’. The mi‑ prefix marks iterative or ongoing aspect; the clause therefore describes a repeated or habitual outcome when in Tahmatan’s company.
segments.s161-g142: گهی با زواره گزیدی نشست again begins with the adverb گهی ‘sometimes’. با زواره is the comitative/locative phrase ‘with/at Zowara’. گزیدی is a past form of گزیدن ‘to choose’ (here a perfect/past participial/finite verb meaning ‘chose’), and نشست is the past verb ‘sat/settled’. The sequence گزیدی نشست is a common classical pattern where one past action (choosing) is given just before the verb of sitting/settling to mean ‘having chosen, he sat’ or ‘he chose and sat down’.
segments.s161-g143: گهی شاد بر تخت دستان بدی is an episodic clause: گهی ‘sometimes’, شاد ‘joyful’ (predicate adjective), بر تخت دستان ‘on Dastan’s throne’ (prepositional locative phrase), and بدی is the predicate verb-form used in this poem to mean ‘was’ (an archaic/poetic form related to بودن). The adjective-predicate + locative phrase + verb order is typical in classical Persian descriptions of temporary states.
segments.s161-g144: گهی در شکار و شبستان بدی parallels the previous clause with گهی ‘sometimes’, followed by two locative phrases joined by و: در شکار ‘in the hunt’ and شبستان ‘in the inner chamber/alcove’. بدی again supplies the copular/verb meaning ‘was’. This produces the alternating picture: sometimes on the throne, sometimes hunting or in the chamber.
segments.s161-g185: گریزان سپهرم is a noun phrase where the participle گریزان (“fleeing”) modifies سپهرم (“my sky”); the possessive -م is the enclitic on سپهرم. The following ب + دان together function as the classical compound بدان (به + آن) meaning “to that / toward that.” روی آب = “the face/surface of the water” (روی = face/surface, آب = water). The whole segment therefore reads literally “the fleeing my‑sky to that face of water,” i.e. “my fleeing sky turned its face toward that water.”
segments.s161-g186: This is poetic/contracted word order: بشدبا represents a sequence of elements built from شد (past of شدن, “became/went”) with the preposition با (“with”), i.e. بشد با = “he went/arrived with.” سپه is the direct accompaniment “the host/army,” and نزد + افراسیاب places the action in the presence/near Afrasiab. Verb precedes its accompaniment here because of compact poetic phrasing.
segments.s161-g187: در بلخ شد uses شد (past of شدن) with a locative phrase to mean “arrived/was in Balkh” (classical Persian often uses شدن with a place to indicate arrival). با سپاه follows to indicate accompaniment: “with the army.” Word order is subject – locative/verb – accompaniment in this poetic line.
segments.s161-g188: یکی نامه فرمود is “he sent/composed a certain/a particular letter.” یکی functions as “a certain” (indefinite modifier). نامه is the object “letter.” فرمود is the classical perf. used here to mean ‘caused/sent/expressed’ (in Epic usage it commonly marks sending or issuing a message). نزدیک شاه places the letter to/near the king (i.e. addressed to the king / delivered to the royal presence).
segments.s161-g189: نوشتن here is the verbal noun/infinitive used as the action “(the) writing” or simply “he wrote.” به is instrumental/with (“with/using”), introducing the materials/ingredients: مشک و گلاب و عبیر (musk, rosewater, and perfume). The conjunction و links the list items in the normal way.
segments.s161-g190: چنان‌چون سزاوار introduces the manner: چنان‌چون = “just as / in the manner that,” سزاوار = “fitting/appropriate.” The following بد in this line is a contracted/poetic form not serving the usual negative meaning; it functions here in connection with بر حریر to indicate placement upon silk (the precise lexical identity is archaic/poetic). بر حریر is literal “on silk.” Overall: “in the manner appropriate, upon silk.”
segments.s161-g191: نخست is adverbial “first/first of all.” آفرین here is a noun ‘praise/acclamation’ (the phrase آفرین کرد = “he offered praise,” where کرد is the light verb “did/made”). بر کردگار is the prepositional target “unto the Creator/God.” The construction is a common classical formula: “first he made praise to the Creator.”
segments.s161-g192: کزو = از + او (from whom / from which) refers back to کردگار (“from whom”). گشت پیروز = “became victorious” (گشت = became, پیروز = victorious). و به روزگار is a short phrase meaning “and in fortune/time” (elliptical for “and prospered in his days/fortune”); the line links divine favor to the hero’s success.
segments.s161-g49: که is a subordinating particle introducing a purpose/complement clause ('that' / 'so that'). با + شاه توران marks the opponent: با = 'with' and شاه توران = 'the king of Turan' (possessive/name phrase follows the preposition). بجویم is 1st person singular present-subjunctive/optative (classical Persian -یم) from جست/جو 'to seek', giving the sense 'may I seek' / 'I should seek'; نبرد is the object 'battle' and sits next to the verb in this poetic order (verb-centered clause).
segments.s161-g50: سر سروران = 'the heads of the chiefs/lords' (سر = heads, سروران = lords). اندر here functions adverbially/prepositionally 'among / in' (classical usage). آرم is an optative/first-person-form (here functioning in the same jussive flavour as بجویم) — poetically 'may I set / may I place / may I rest', used with به گرد ('around') to indicate placing the heads around. به گرد is an adverbial phrase 'around/about' (به + گرد). The whole clause continues the speaker's purpose: to place the chiefs' heads around (him/there).
segments.s161-g51: چنین 'thus/so' is an adverb opening the clause. بود is the past copula 'was' (here placing the statement in past narrative). رای 'counsel/plan' is the noun being predicated, modified by جهان‌آفرین 'the world‑creator / Creator' (a divine epithet). Word order here is emphatic: 'Thus was the Creator's counsel.'
segments.s161-g52: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of that counsel. او 'he' is the subject. جان 'life' is the direct object. سپارد is a verb meaning 'to deliver/leave/entrust' (here 'to give up/deliver [one's life]'), in the narrative past/optative sense describing what will happen. به توران زمین = 'to/into the land of Turan' (به + place; زمین = 'land / country').
segments.s161-g53: به رای و به اندیشه نابکار = 'by (the) counsel and by (the) wicked intent'. The preposition به here marks manner/instrument ('by' or '-ly'), repeated for rhetorical parallelism. اندیشه نابکار is a noun + adjective phrase 'wicked thought/intent' (نابکار postmodifies اندیشه).
segments.s161-g54: کجا 'where' introduces the question/rhetorical exclamation. بازگردد is a present/subjunctive or potential form 'may return / will return' (باز + گردد), here used interrogatively. بد روزگار = 'ill‑fortune / bad days' (بد qualifying روزگار); روزگار has a fate/time meaning, so the phrase means roughly 'ill‑fate' or 'evil fortune'.
segments.s161-g55: بدین کار = 'by this affair / in this matter' (بدین = با + این, classical fused form, meaning 'with/by this'). همداستان شد is a compound predicate meaning 'became like‑minded / agreed' (شد = past 'became'). پدر is the subject 'the father'. The phrase thus says 'the father agreed to this plan.'
segments.s161-g56: که introduces the content of that agreement ('that ...'). بندد is a present-subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person form from بست/بند 'to bind' here used with future/purpose flavour 'he should bind / he will bind'. برین is orthographically 'بر این' (بر = 'on/upon', ین = demonstrative 'this'), so 'upon this'. کین 'vengeance/enmity' modifies کمر (a girdle) — 'a girdle of vengeance'. سیاوش کمر is a genitive/possessive sequence 'Siyâvash's waist' (name + body part); word order places the possessor before the possessed in classical Persian poetic style, giving 'bind ... upon Siyâvash's waist.'
segments.s161-g249: فرستاده (subject) + نزد سیاوش (prepositional phrase 'to/at the presence of Siavash') + رسید (past tense verb 'arrived/reached'). Persian typically places the verb at the end; here the action 'reached' is in the simple past and the agent (the messenger) is explicit.
segments.s161-g250: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; آن نامه شاه ایران is a noun phrase 'that letter of the King of Iran' (آن determiner + نامه + genitive compound شاه ایران), and بدید is the past tense 'saw'. The subject is implied (Siavash), so the clause means 'when he saw that letter...'.
segments.s161-g251: زمین را uses را as the definite object marker ('the earth'); ببوسید is past 'he kissed'. The clause continues with و (and) followed by دل شاد کرد, where کرد is the causative/auxiliary 'made' and شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy/joyful' applied to دل ('heart') — literally 'made the heart joyful.'
segments.s161-g252: ز هر غم is a prepositional phrase 'from every sorrow' (ز = 'from'); دل‌پاک is the direct object 'the pure‑hearted (one)' and آزاد کرد is 'freed' or 'made free' (کرد as causative/performative verb). Word order places the source ('from every sorrow') before the object and verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s161-g253: ازان نامه شاه places the cause/source first ('from that letter, the king...'); چون here functions as 'since/because' (a causal link), گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became' and شاد is the resultative adjective 'joyful.' So the clause means 'because of that letter, the king became joyful.'
segments.s161-g254: بخندید is simple past 'he laughed.' The coordinate clause نامه بسر بر نهاد is a light-verb/compound expression meaning 'he placed/laid the letter (away/aside)'; بر نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to put/place' with بر 'upon,' and this idiomatic sequence is literary — نامه is the direct object.
segments.s161-g255: نگه داشت is a compound where نگه ('watch/keeping') combines with داشت (here 'kept/was keeping') to mean 'kept watch' or 'remained vigilant.' بیدار is an adjectival complement 'awake/alert.' فرمان اوی is a possessive phrase 'his command/order' (اوی = his/her). Word order: object/phrase فرمان اوی follows the predicate, a poetic arrangement.
segments.s161-g256: نپیچید is the negative past of پیچید ('did not turn/deviate'). دل را has را as the definite object marker ('the heart'); ز پیمان اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from his covenant/promise' (ز = from, اوی = his). Together: 'he did not turn his heart away from his pledge.'
segments.s161-g193: This noun phrase names the subject: خداوند (a sovereign/lord) followed by two coordinating noun phrases 'خورشید و گردنده ماه' (the sun and the circling moon). Note the classical order: head noun 'خداوند' comes first, then its appositive list. 'گردنده' is a participial adjective modifying 'ماه' ('the moon that turns/rotates').
segments.s161-g194: فرازنده is a participle/agentive describing the subject ("exalter" or "he who raises/upholds"). It governs a coordinated direct object list 'تاج و تخت و کلاه' (crown, throne, and cap/hat). The particle و simply links the items in the list.
segments.s161-g195: 'کسی را که خواهد برآرد بلند' is a relative/conditional phrase: 'کسی را که' = 'whom' (object marked by را), 'خواهد' expresses desire/will, and 'برآرد بلند' is a compound verb phrase meaning 'raises up, brings high' (برآرد = bring up/raise; بلند = high/lofty). Word order places the object/relative first, then the verb phrase.
segments.s161-g196: This balanced clause contrasts with the previous: 'یکی را' ('one/another' as object, marked by را), 'کند' is the causative/do verb 'makes/puts', followed by the predicates 'سوگوار و نژند' (mourning/grieved and base/worthless). Persian often uses کند + adjective to mean 'makes [someone] X.'
segments.s161-g197: The clause uses a correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' (neither ... nor ...). 'به فرمانش اندر' = 'by/under his command' (به + فرمانش + اندر 'in/within'), and 'چون' here means 'as' or 'in the way that' and links to the following clause about wisdom. 'چرا' appears as an exclamatory/rhetorical particle introducing the negative construction (classical usage approximating 'indeed/why').
segments.s161-g198: خرد (a noun, 'wisdom') is governed by کرد (past tense of 'to do/act') to mean 'acted with wisdom' or 'behaved wisely.' باید is the modal 'must/ought,' and بدین is the combination of prefix ب- + دین (here functioning as 'by/with this' in the classical phrasing), followed by رهنمون 'guide.' The clause therefore says one ought to follow/ be guided by this guidance; note that Persian can omit an explicit finite verb after باید when context makes it clear.
segments.s161-g199: ازان (from that/of that) introduces the epithet 'دادگر' (a just one / just ruler), followed by the archaic relative/particle kaو (classical 'کاو' = 'who' = که او) linking to the verb phrase 'جهان آفرید' ('created the world'). Note the classical word order: epithet + relative + object + past verb.
segments.s161-g200: This clause lists what was created. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/manifest,' نهان 'hidden,' and آفرید is the past verb 'created.' Grammatically the verb follows the items it governs, a common classical order; the first word 'ابا' in the line does not match the given lexical senses and is here treated as a textual/poetic particle (sense not provided), while the core meaning is 'He created the manifest and the hidden.'
segments.s161-g97: This is a fronted prepositional phrase listing origins: هم 'also' + از 'from' + a series of place/people names joined by و. Word order places the preposition before the list. The conjunctions و simply join the items; the segment functions as one coordinated 'from X, Y, Z' phrase modifying an omitted verb in the larger passage.
segments.s161-g98: ز here is the poetic variant of از 'from'; it begins a second coordinated source phrase: 'from Gilan ... and the plain of Sarvach.' جنگی is used as a substantive/adjective meaning 'fighters/warriors' or 'warlike ones' and دشت سروچ is a place-name ('plain of Sarvach'). The two coordinated parts are linked to the same omitted verb in the larger sentence.
segments.s161-g99: This noun phrase names a leader (سپرور) followed by a specification of troops: پیاده 'infantry/foot soldiers' and the numeral phrase ده و دو هزار (poetic ordering) meaning 'twelve thousand.' Persian epic often places numerals after the noun; the conjunction و here simply connects elements within the noun phrase.
segments.s161-g100: گزین کرد is a past-tense compound: گزین 'choose' + کرد 'did' → 'chose/selected.' In classical/poetic Persian the subject شاه follows the verb (V S word order for emphasis). از در کارزار is a locative/source phrase 'from/in the field of battle' (از = from; در = in; کارزار = battlefield) that indicates where the selection came from.
segments.s161-g101: This starts with a topical 'از ایران' ('from Iran'), followed by the indefinite relative construction هرآنکس که 'whoever that...' The predicate in this clause is گوزاده بود — گوزاده here appears as a family/name-form or attribute and بود is the past copula 'was.' The relative clause establishes the set of people being described.
segments.s161-g102: This is a coordinated adjectival predicate applying to the antecedent of the relative clause: three adjectives joined by و and closed by the copula بود at the end. In Persian the final بود governs all preceding adjectives: 'was brave and wise and noble.'
segments.s161-g103: به here introduces a locative/temporal phrase: به بالا 'in high rank' (بالا = high/lofty) coordinated with سال سیاوش 'the year/time of Siyavash.' The plural past verb بدند follows and agrees with the people described; this is a typical poetic order (locative/time phrase → verb → predicate).
segments.s161-g104: A three-term adjectival predicate (خردمند 'wise', بیدار 'alert/awake', خامش 'silent') joined by و, with the past plural copula بدند at the end applying to all three adjectives — 'they were wise, alert, and silent.'
segments.s161-g177: نگه کرد is a simple past verbal phrase meaning 'looked' (literally 'made a look'); classical Persian often places the verb before the subject. گرسیوز جنگ‌جوی follows the verb as the subject plus epithet/apposition: گرسیوز (proper name) with the descriptive noun جنگ‌جوی ('warrior') attached after the name.
segments.s161-g178: This clause contains a negated perception: جز از ... ندید ایچ روی = 'he saw nothing except ... on the face'. جز از ('except/from') introduces the limited content, جستن is a verbal noun 'seeking' or 'to seek', and ندید is past 'did not see' used with ایچ ('nothing') to give 'saw nothing'. روی here is 'face' and is the locus where that quality appears.
segments.s161-g179: چو = 'when'. ز ایران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'the army from Iran' (ز is the poetic form of از). اندر آمد = 'entered' or 'came in'; classical Persian often separates the location/tempus phrase, so آمد (past 'came') is followed by the destination/locative phrase به تنگ 'to the narrow [pass]'. تنگ here means a narrow passage/defile.
segments.s161-g180: به دروازه بلخ is the locative phrase 'at the gate of Balkh' (به + place), and برخاست جنگ is an inversion where the verb برخاست 'arose/broke out' comes before its subject جنگ 'battle' — literally 'battle rose' = 'battle broke out'.
segments.s161-g181: دو جنگ گران is a noun phrase 'two heavy/fierce battles' (عدد + noun + adjective). کرده شد is a passive/perfect-like periphrasis meaning 'were made/occurred' (کرده 'done' + شد 'happened'), and در سه روز is the time expression 'in/within three days'. The whole yields 'two fierce battles were fought in three days.'
segments.s161-g182: بیامد is past 'came/arrived' (verb placed before subject here). سیاووش is the subject (proper name), and لشکر فروز is an appositive phrase 'the shining/glorious army' where فروز modifies لشکر; together: 'Siyavash arrived with his glorious army.'
segments.s161-g183: پیاده فرستاد = 'he sent (the) infantry/foot-soldiers' — پیاده used as a noun 'infantry' or 'foot-soldiers' and فرستاد is past 'sent'. بر هر دری is a destination phrase 'to/at every door/gate' (بر هر = 'to each'); note that دری here is an archaic/poetic form for 'در' (gate).
segments.s161-g184: به بلخ اندر آمد = 'came into Balkh' (به + place + اندر آمد 'entered/came in'). گران لشکری is a nominal phrase 'a heavy/formidable army' (گران modifying لشکری), so the clause reads 'a formidable army entered Balkh.'
segments.s161-g225: همیشه is an adverb 'always'; به introduces the beneficiary/possessor 'for/to' (here 'belonging to'); پیروزی و فرهی are coordinated nouns ('victory and nobility') that function as the recipients introduced by به. The clause is elliptical: the copular/optative verb ('may there be') is omitted and supplied by the sentence as a whole.
segments.s161-g226: کلاه and تاج are the two coordinated noun phrases (a 'cap' and a 'crown') that complete the wish implied by the preceding prepositional phrase; بزرگی here describes the hat as large/great (a noun used descriptively), and مهی is a poetic adjective 'moon‑like/bright' modifying تاج. The optative sense ('may there be') is still understood from the context.
segments.s161-g227: سپه بردی is a past‑tense 2nd‑person clause: سپه (army/host) precedes the verb بردی ('you took/led'), so 'you raised/led an army.' و links this clause to the next; جنگ را خواستی uses the object marker را on جنگ ('war/battle') and خواستی is past 'you wanted/sought' — 'you sought war.'
segments.s161-g228: که here gives the causal sense 'for/because'; بخت و هنر داری uses داری (present 'you have') to predicate possession of two coordinated nouns ('fortune/luck and skill/art'). The final و راستی adds 'and uprightness/truth' to the list of qualities governed by داری (the verb applies to all three nouns).
segments.s161-g229: همی (an emphatic/adverbial 'still') begins the clause; از لبت is a locative/ablative phrase 'from/at your lip' (i.e. 'on your lips'); شیر is the subject 'the lion' (metaphoric for courage) and بوید (present/past poetic form of بوییدن) here means 'gives off/emits (a scent)' — together: 'still the lion's scent is on your lip.' هنوز reinforces the 'still/yet' sense.
segments.s161-g230: که is a relative 'which/that' linking this clause to the previous image; زد is the past of زدن 'struck/struck against' and takes the phrase بر کمان تو 'upon your bow' (تو = your). از جنگ توز gives the source/time 'in/at the battle of Tuz' (توز here a proper/archaic name). The clause describes the occasion that produced the lion's scent on the lips.
segments.s161-g231: همیشه is 'always'; هنرمند is used predicatively 'skillful/artful'; بادا is the classical optative particle 'may it be' (expressing a wish), and تنت ('your body') is the grammatical patient/subject of the wish, placed after the optative (a poetic word order). The phrase literally: 'Always skillful may be your body.'
segments.s161-g232: رسیده is a past participle 'reached/attained' used as predicate (the optative sense is carried from بادا to this clause); به کام = 'to the fulfilment/pleasure of' so رسیده به کام means 'have reached satisfaction'; دل روشنت is 'your bright/clear heart' with the possessive ـت attached to روشن (poetic ordering). Together: 'may your bright heart have its desire fulfilled.'
segments.s161-g241: مکن is the classical negative imperative 'do not'. Here it governs the following verbal notion expressed by the infinitive جستن ('to seek'); هیچ intensifies the negation ('at all/never'). The phrase بر جنگ جستن is an infinitival complement meaning 'to seek war' (بر = toward/against + جنگ = war). شتاب is a noun 'haste' used with مکن to mean 'do not be hasty' or 'do not hasten to...'. Word order is emphatic/poetic: negative imperative + intensifier + prepositional infinitive + noun of manner.
segments.s161-g242: به جنگ (به + noun) marks motion/tendency 'into/against battle'. آید is a narrative/present-future-form of آمدن 'to come' (here: 'will come' / 'shall come'), and خود is emphatic 'himself'. تو is the 2nd‑person object 'you'. The subject (افراسیاب) follows for emphasis: 'Afrasiab himself will come against you.'
segments.s161-g243: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small temporal/particle ('even now' / 'at this very time') used in older/poetic Persian. زین روی is a compact phrase where زین ('saddle, bridle, trappings') and روی ('on/over' or 'side') combine to mean 'from this saddle/bridle' or 'by this tack'; جیحون is the river (Amu Darya). کشد here is a classical third‑person singular of کشیدن 'to pull/drag' used in a conditional sense 'should pull/drag'. (Note: in the provided sense list the usual 'to pull' sense is not listed; the verb here functions as a simple action verb 'pulls/draws'.)
segments.s161-g244: همی is a narrative/adverbial particle marking continuous or emphatic action ('then/indeed/still'); دامن خویش is a possessive phrase 'his own hem/skirt' (دامن = hem/robe, خویش = 'his own' formed from خوی + ش), and در خون کشد uses کشد ('to drag/drag along') with در خون 'in/into blood' to mean 'he will (so) drag his hem in blood' — i.e. he will be drenched/stained in blood. The construction expresses consequence: if X pulls the river, Y will end up bloody.
segments.s161-g245: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'put/placed' used here in a verbal sense 'he put/laid' or more idiomatically 'he set aside / showed' (in older Persian it can indicate causing or bestowing). از بر (lit. 'from upon') is a prepositional phrase that, together with the following noun phrase, expresses the object affected. نامه‌بر is 'the messenger/envoy'. مهر خویش is 'his own kindness/favor' (مهر = affection/favor; خویش is the possessive 'his own' realized as خوی + ش). Taken together, the phrase indicates he acted toward the messenger with his own kindness (i.e. showed/extended favour).
segments.s161-g246: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment / immediately'. فرستاده is 'the messenger'; را marks it as the definite object. خواند here is one of its classical meanings 'to call/summon' (not just 'to read'), so فرستاده را خواند پیش = 'he summoned the messenger before him'. پیش, postposed, means 'in (his) presence' or 'before (him)'.
segments.s161-g247: بدو is 'to him' (به + او contracted in classical usage) meaning 'he gave to him'. داد is the past 'gave'. و is the coordinating 'and'. فرمود is the past of فرمایدن, here 'he commanded/said (to him)'; تا introduces a purposive or result clause 'so that/that', and گشت باز uses گشت (turned/returned) + باز 'back/again' to mean 'he turned back/returned'. The clause thus reports: 'He gave it to him and commanded (him) so that he would return.'
segments.s161-g248: همی is the narrative particle again ('then/so'); تاخت is the past of تاختن 'to gallop/rush' (here 'he rode/galloped'). اندر is a classical preposition 'in/within', and نشیب و فراز is a fixed pair 'downhill and uphill' (nāp/low and height/top) meaning 'up and down, over the slopes'. So the clause describes swift riding up and down the terrain.
segments.s161-g153: سوی (preposition) + place + آمد (past tense) gives a motion clause 'came toward X'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. مرورود here is a proper name/toponym placed after the conjunction as the next subject/topic of the line.
segments.s161-g154: سپهرش is سپهر + possessive -ش 'his/their heavens' (here 'the heavens' or poetic domain). همی is the older continuous/iterative marker (classical Persian) meaning 'kept on / would'. داد is the simple past 'gave' used transitively. گفتی درود is a noun phrase 'a spoken greeting' (گفتی = 'speech/utterance', درود = 'greeting'); together they are the direct object of داد (i.e. 'gave/offered a verbal greeting').
segments.s161-g155: ازانپس is a temporal adverb 'from then on / afterwards'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به + نزدیک + بلخ = a locative phrase 'to/near Balkh' where به expresses motion/toward and نزدیک qualifies the following place name.
segments.s161-g156: نیازرد here functions as a classical negative verb (نی‑ + آزارَد) meaning 'did not harm / did not hurt'; this is an older contracted form rather than the proper-name reading. کس را is the indefinite object 'anyone' with the object marker را. به in this clause means 'by/with' (instrumental), connecting to گفتار تلخ 'bitter speech' (گفتار = speech, تلخ = figuratively harsh).
segments.s161-g157: وزان is a poetic contraction of و + از آن 'and from that/thereupon' introducing consequence. روی here is literal 'face/side' but in this formula functions idiomatically to mark the circumstance ('on that account / therefore'); گرسیوز and بارمان are proper names joined by و.
segments.s161-g158: کشیدند is the past plural 'they drew/dragged/advanced' (here 'marshaled/moved forth'). لشکر is the direct object 'army'. چو is the comparative particle 'like/as'. باد دمان is a simile 'wind of the hem/skirt' (دَمان = skirt/foothill edge) — together giving the image 'they moved like a wind along the skirts/slopes.'
segments.s161-g159: سپهرم here is best read as a proper name (Sepahram) rather than the literal 'my sky'. بد is an adjectival comment 'bad/unfavorable' or may belong to an ancient compound/title; in the line the pair 'سپهرم بد و بارمان' functions as two names (or a name + epithet) joined by و. پیش رو is a prepositional phrase 'in front / at the fore' (پیش = before/in front, رو as 'face/toward').
segments.s161-g160: خبر شد is an impersonal/resultative construction 'it became news / news came' (شد as auxiliary/verb 'happened/was made'). بدیشان = به + ایشان 'to them' (the dative). ز here is the poetic genitive/preposition 'about/from', سالار نو = 'the new commander' (سالار = commander, نو = new); together 'news reached them about the new commander.'
segments.s161-g73: همی before a verb is an archaic/poetic marker of continued or habitual action ('still/keeps on'). خواهد is the 3rd-person present/future of خواستن ('wants'/'desires'). او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'. جنگ افراسیاب is the object phrase 'war with (or against) Afrasiab' — in poetry the linking particle (ezāfe or با) is often omitted, so the two nouns stand together to mean 'war with Afrasiab.'
segments.s161-g74: This is a string of imperatives/instructions. تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. با او is an accompanying prepositional phrase 'with him'. برو is the imperative 'go'. روی here functions as an instruction to 'turn (your) face; face' (imperative sense), and ازو marks the target ('toward/at him' in older usage). برمتاب is an archaic imperative formed from بر + متاب(یدن) meaning 'smite/assail' (directed toward the preceding target). The sequence gives: 'You, go with him; face him and strike at him.'
segments.s161-g75: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when' (or 'if'). بیدار باشی uses the present/subjunctive form باشی (second person) with the adjective بیدار, yielding 'when/if you are awake' — note the subject pronoun تو follows the predicate, a common classical/orderly inversion. خواب آیدم uses آیدم (I come) with خواب as subject, an idiom meaning 'sleep comes to me' (i.e. 'I fall asleep').
segments.s161-g76: Again چو = 'when'. آرام یابی has یابی (second-person present/subjunctive of یافتن) 'you find/attain' with آرام 'calm/peace', so 'when you find peace.' شتاب آیدم repeats the idiom pattern with آیدم ('comes to me') and the noun شتاب 'haste' as subject — 'haste comes to me' (i.e. 'I grow hurried').
segments.s161-g77: جهان is the noun 'the world'. ایمن is a predicate adjective 'safe/protected'. از تیر و شمشیر is a prepositional phrase 'from arrow and sword'. تست here is the archaic poetic copula (from تو + است), meaning 'is (by) you' or more naturally 'because of you' — the structure says that the world is safe by/through you. Note poetic word order placing predicate before the copula.
segments.s161-g78: سر ماه is a poetic metaphor: سر 'head/top' + ماه 'moon' — 'your head (is) the moon' (i.e. radiant/beautiful). با چرخ 'with the sky/heaven' and در زیر تست places things under the subject: در + زیر together give 'under/in beneath' and تست again is the archaic copula 'thou art' — overall: '(your) head is the moon, and the heavens are beneath you.' The line uses inverted/condensed classical order for poetic effect.
segments.s161-g79: تهمتن is the speaker's name/title. بدو is the dative phrase 'to him' (بد + او contracted). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past tense). من بنده‌ام is a formulaic humble self-reference: من = 'I' and بنده‌ام = بنده + -ام 'I am a servant' (here used as modesty formula 'I am your servant').
segments.s161-g80: سخن is 'speech/words'. هرچ + گویی is a common classical pattern: هرچ 'whatever' and گویی (subjunctive/imperative sense of گفتن here) yields 'whatever you say'. نیوشنده‌ام is بنده/agent noun نیوشنده 'listener' with the first-person copular suffix -ام: 'I am a listener' or 'I will listen.' Together: 'I will hear/listen to whatever you say.'
