segments.s161-g169: تو is a vocative/2nd‑person pronoun ('thou/you') addressing someone. لشکر بیاری is a noun phrase in which بیاری (from یار) functions as 'companion/friend' modifying لشکر, so the phrase reads 'army‑companion' or 'friend of the army' rather than a verbal 'bring the army.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' چندین is an indefinite numeral/adjective 'several.' مپای is an obscure/rare noun left untranslated here (poetic term), placed after چندین as its head.
segments.s161-g170: که introduces a relative or result clause 'which/that.' از باد is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the wind' (باد = wind). کشتی is the noun 'ship,' and بجنبد is a present/subjunctive form of جنبیدن ('to move/stir') expressing potential/result ('may move' or 'is set in motion'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' and جای is 'place'; together بجنبد ز جای = 'move from its place.'
segments.s161-g171: برانگیخت is a third‑person past verb 'he roused/awoke.' برسان here is an imperative/jussive form of رساندن or a second verb in sequence meaning 'he brought/sent (forth).' آتش is the noun 'fire,' and هیون (هیَوَن) is a poetic noun 'beast/monster' modifying آتش (genitive relation implied without ezāfe): 'the beast's fire' or 'the monster's fiery fury.' The two verbs are juxtaposed in classical poetic style.
segments.s161-g172: کزین سان is a fixed phrase: کزین ('from this') + سان ('manner/like') = 'in this way/thus.' سخن راند is an idiomatic compound where سخن ('speech/words') is the object and راند (past of راندن 'to drive') here means 'uttered/spoke' (poetic use). با is the instrumental preposition 'with,' and رهنمون is a noun 'guidance/guide,' so با رهنمون indicates the manner or means ('with guiding words' or 'by means of guidance').
segments.s161-g173: سیاووش is the proper name and subject. زین سو is an adverbial/locative phrase 'on this side/from here' (classical phrasing). به پاسخ is a prepositional phrase 'for an answer' or 'to answer,' where به marks purpose. نماند is the third‑person past of نماندن meaning 'did not remain' (here 'he did not stay/wait to reply').
segments.s161-g174: سوی بلخ is the directional phrase 'toward Balkh' (سوی = toward(s); بلخ = Balkh). چون introduces a simile 'like/as.' باد is 'wind,' لشکر 'army/host,' and براند is a third‑person form of راندن 'to drive' (here rendered as 'he drove' — in poetic narrative it carries the simple past/historical sense). The clause describes moving the army toward Balkh as swiftly as the wind.
segments.s161-g175: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' تنگ here denotes a narrow place/pass (تنگ = defile). اندر آمد is the verbal phrase 'came in/entered' (اندر = in; آمد = came). ز ایران سپاه is a genitival/attributive sequence 'the army of Iran' or 'the Iranian army' (ز used poetically here as 'of/from'). Word order in classical Persian often places such modifiers after the verb.
segments.s161-g176: نشایست is a past (stative) adjective/verb 'it was not fitting/was unseemly' (ن- prefix negates شایست). کردن is the light verb/infinitival auxiliary 'to do' often used after evaluative adjectives (here forming 'to befit/to do X'). به پاسخ نگاه is an infinitival/complement phrase 'to answer with a look' (به marks manner, پاسخ = answer, نگاه = glance/gaze). The whole phrase means 'it was not fitting to answer by looking.'
segments.s161-g217: This clause contains an exhortation + a short result clause. به + noun (به یزدان) is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/with God' and here marks the object/goal of the verb. پناهید is a polite/old-fashioned imperative or hortative ('take refuge' / 'seek refuge'); و simply links the next clause. ز (poetic از) means 'from', and جست is an intransitive perfect/simple past meaning 'leapt/sprang' (here 'sprang forth'); بخت is the subject 'fortune/lot'.
segments.s161-g218: بدان is the imperative 'know' formed from the prefix ب- + دان (be- + dān), so the split here shows the prefix + verb-stem; تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that', followed by ببار آید, where ببار ('to pour/rain') with آید ('may come') yields a jussive/subjunctive sense 'may rain/come to pour'. آن نو درخت is the object of that clause: 'that new tree' (نو درخت = sapling/new tree).
segments.s161-g219: به شادی is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'with joy' (به used adverbially 'by/with'). یکی نامه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain letter' (یکی = 'a/some'), followed by پاسخ نوشت — a simple past clause 'wrote an answer' (نوشت = 'he wrote'). Word order is adverbial phrase + object + verb (common in classical Persian).
segments.s161-g220: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'. تازه بهاری is a nominal phrase 'fresh/springlike' describing the comparison, and در اردیبهشت means 'in Ordibehesht' (the month). The whole phrase modifies the preceding clause: 'like fresh spring in Ordibehesht.'
segments.s161-g221: که is a relative pronoun 'who/which/that' linking to a following description. از آفریننده is 'from/the Creator' (آفریننده = 'creator'), and هور و ماه names two celestial figures ('Hūr' / 'splendor' and 'moon' — a poetic pair). The preposition از marks origin/agency here.
segments.s161-g222: This continues the epithets describing that divine being: جهاندار is an honorific 'world-ruler/sovereign'; و links to بخشنده 'bestower/giver' (here active agent meaning 'bestower'); تاج و گاه are the objects of giving — 'crown and time' (گاه here is a poetic noun 'time/occasion'). The construction is nominal apposition — a string of titles.
segments.s161-g223: ترا is the bound object form (ت- + را) meaning 'to you' (object/beneficiary). جاودان شادمان باد is an optative formula: جاودان (eternal) + شادمان (joyful) + باد (optative particle 'may it be'), and دل is the noun 'heart' — overall: 'May your heart be eternally joyful.'
segments.s161-g224: ز introduces cause/source 'from/out of' (ز درد و بلا = 'from pain and calamity'). گشته is a perfect/participial form of شدن 'become' ('has become' / 'is become'), here meaning 'has been made/freed', and آزاد دل repeats 'free (is) the heart' — syntactically a verbless clause relying on participle, i.e. 'the heart has become free from pain and calamity.'
segments.s161-g257: وزان سو: a short, somewhat poetic locative phrase meaning 'from that side/direction' — وزان (compound prepositional/relational word) + سو ('side'). چو here introduces the following noun phrase as the (simile or identification) of who comes; the name and epithets follow the particle in the order چو + name + adjective + noun (چو گرسیوز شوخ مرد = 'Garsivaz, the impudent man').
segments.s161-g258: بیامد is simple past 'came'. بر شاه ترکان is the prepositional object 'toward/on the king of the Turks' (بر here = 'toward/to' + شاه ترکان). چو گرد at the end is a simile 'like a گرد' (گرد = swirling dust/whirlwind), so the clause describes the manner of coming ('came ... like a whirlwind').
segments.s161-g259: بگفت is past tense 'he said' (simple narrative). آن سخنهای ناپاک و تلخ is the direct object: آن + سخنهای (plural 'words/speeches') modified by the coordinated adjectives ناپاک (morally impure/insulting) و تلخ ('bitter' in the figurative sense). Word order is determiner + noun + adjectives.
segments.s161-g260: که آمد here introduces a subordinate temporal/relative clause 'when/that came' (که used as a conjunction). آمد is past 'arrived'; سپهبد سیاوش به بلخ names the arriving person (the title سپهبد + name سیاوش) and the destination بلخ introduced by به ('to/into'). The whole subordinate clause gives the time/context for the speaking.
segments.s161-g261: سپه کش is a compound nominal: سپه ('army') + کش (agentive element, 'one who draws/commands'), so 'army-leader/commander'. چو رستم is a simile 'like Rostam' (چو = 'like/as'); سپاهی گران is a noun + adjective ('سپاهی' soldier/host, 'گران' heavy/weighty = formidable host). Word order places the simile immediately after the agentive noun.
segments.s161-g262: بسی here functions as an intensifier meaning 'very/many' (classical usage), followed by a coordinated noun phrase نامداران و جنگ آوران 'renowned ones and war-bringers' (نامداران = famed men, جنگ‌آوران = fighters/warriors). The adjectives/nouns are in the plural and listed after the quantifier.
segments.s161-g263: ز هر یک ز ما بود پنجاه بیش is a compact numerical statement: ز = 'from', هر یک = 'each one', ز ما = 'of us' (poetic doubled preposition), بود = past 'there was/were', پنجاه بیش = 'more than fifty' (بیش following the numeral). The phrase literally reads 'from each one of us there was more than fifty'.
segments.s161-g264: سرافراز با گرزه گاومیش is a post-numeral descriptive phrase: سرافراز ('proud/triumphant') modifying the men, با ('with') introduces the weapon گرزه ('mace'), specified as گاومیش ('(made of) water-buffalo' — i.e., buffalo-mace). The preposition با links the men to the instrument they bear.
segments.s161-g57: ازو is the prepositional/oblique pronoun 'because of him / from him' placed before the predicate for emphasis. شادمان گشت is an adjectival predication: adjective + گشت (past of گشتن used as a copula) = 'became glad'. و simply links the two past actions. بنواختش is the past form of نواختن with the object clitic -ش (him/it) attached; in classical Persian نواختن can mean 'to play (music)' or 'to strike/perform', so here it functions as a transitive past verb 'he played/struck for him'.
segments.s161-g58: به marks the beneficiary/recipient ('for' or 'to'), followed by نوی یکی پایگه which is an archaic/poetic ordering: 'نوی' (new) precedes 'یکی پایگه' ('a pedestal/base'). ساختش is ساخت + object clitic -ش and is the past tense 'he built/made (it/for him)'; the object/clitic refers back to the beneficiary mentioned (i.e. 'for him').
segments.s161-g59: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e. 'said to him'). گفت here is the past-reporting verb 'said' (the provided lexeme senses list treats گفت as a noun, but in context it's the verb گُفت). گنج و گهر are coordinated nouns 'treasure and jewels'. پیش تست is an older idiom: پیش = 'before/in front of', and تست is a contracted archaic copula meaning 'is yours' (modern توست); together 'پیشِ توست' = 'are before you / are yours'.
segments.s161-g60: تو is the pronoun 'you'. گویی here is the modal/parenthetical 'one might say / it seems / you could say' (classical use of گو + یی-form); the supplied senses treat گویی as a noun, but here it's verbal/modal. سپه 'host/army' is the noun; سر به سر is an idiom 'head by head' or 'entirely' (literally 'head to head'), modifying سپه to stress the completeness of the host. خویش + ش = خویشِ + -ش yields 'his/your own' (own/selves). تست again is the contracted copula 'is' (here 'is yours'). The clause as a whole expresses that the entire army could be regarded as belonging to him.
segments.s161-g61: ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از meaning 'from' or 'because of'. گفتار و کردار are a standard pair 'speech/words and deeds/actions' joined by و. سپس از آفرین: از marks 'of/from' and آفرین here means 'praise/acclamation' (not the verbal root). The whole segment lists the causes/reasons (from your words, deeds, and praise).
segments.s161-g62: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'. خوانند is the classical plural present/potential of خواندن meaning 'they sing/recite' or 'they call out' (here 'they sing/recite [praise]'). بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'about you' or 'upon you'. به ایران‌زمین indicates the location 'in the land of Iran/throughout Iran'. Together the clause says 'that they sing about you in the land of Iran.'
segments.s161-g63: گو here is a narrative/transitional particle 'so/then' (also found as an imperative 'say' in other contexts); it introduces the reported action. پیلتن is a proper name (object), and را is the definite-object marker. بر خویش is literal 'toward himself' (i.e. 'to him / to his presence'). خواند is the past form of خواندن here in the sense 'to call/summon' (he summoned/ sent for Piltan).
segments.s161-g64: بسی is a poetic adverb 'many'/'much' (the provided lexeme senses list does not show the common adverbial sense but here it means 'many'). داستانهای نیکو is the object 'many fine tales/stories' (داستانهای = tales, نیکو = good/fine). براند is an older/presentational verbal form meaning 'he related/produced' in the narrative sense here; the exact modern cognate is not in the supplied sense list, so it functions as the past/result verb 'he told/put forth' these tales.
segments.s161-g209: کنون (adverb) places the time at the start: “now.” تا به جیحون is a prepositional/extent phrase meaning “as far as/to the Jeyhun” (تا … به + place). سپاه is the nominal subject and منست is an archaic copular/possessive phrase here indicating possession or status (roughly “is mine” / “belongs to me”); the verb comes after the locative phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s161-g210: جهان is the subject “the world.” زیر فر کلاه is a stacked prepositional/nominal phrase: زیر + فر (the royal radiance/glory) + کلاه (lit. “cap/hat”), i.e. “under the cap of (one’s) glory.” منست again functions as the predicate/copula in archaic poetic form (“is [mine]/is so”).
segments.s161-g211: به سغد is a locative prepositional phrase “in/to Sogdiana” placed before the verb for emphasis. است is the copula “is/are.” با لشکر افراسیاب is an accompanying/agent phrase “with Afrasiab’s army,” modifying the state expressed by است.
segments.s161-g212: سپاه و سپهبد is a coordinated noun phrase “the troops and the spahbed (commander).” The following ب + دان functions together as an archaic/demonstrative or directional particle (here effectively “to/at that”), and روی آب is a locative expression using روی (“face/surface/onto”) + آب (“water”), so the whole cluster indicates that the troops and commander are oriented/toward the river or present by the water.
segments.s161-g213: گر is the conditional particle “if.” ایدونک is an emphatic/temporal particle (poetic/archaic) meaning roughly “at this moment” or “now.” فرمان دهد is a verb phrase “gives/orders” (دهد is the present/subjunctive/optative third-person form), and شهریار is the subject placed after the verb, a common poetic order: “if the king gives the command.”
segments.s161-g214: سپه is the direct object/affected group “the army.” بگذرانم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form meaning “I will cause/pass/lead (them) through” (here: lead or bring the army into action). کنم is an auxiliary/first‑person verb “I will do/make,” used here with کارزار (the noun “battle”) as the action that will be performed: “I will lead the army and carry out the battle.”
segments.s161-g215: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” نامه‌بر is the agent “the messenger/envoy.” شاه ایران is a genitive construction “the King of Iran,” and رسید is the past tense verb “arrived/reached,” placed at the end in typical poetic word order: “when the messenger reached the King of Iran.”
segments.s161-g216: سر تاج و تختش is a coordinated nominal phrase: سر (here “top/head/summit”) of تاج (“crown”) and تختش (“his throne,” with the possessive suffix -ش). به کیوان رسید is a stative/result clause “reached Saturn” (Kiwan = Saturn), an astrological/poetic locution indicating that the crown and throne came under Saturn’s influence; رسید is the past-tense verb.
segments.s161-g265: پیاده functions adverbially/nominatively here: 'on foot'. به کردار آتش is a simile 'in the manner of fire' (به + noun + کردار = 'in the manner/way of'). بدند is an archaic/poetic past-plural verbal form placed after the phrase, meaning 'they were/they moved' (here: 'they advanced/charged'); the clause order is verb-final but preserves the simile modifier before the verb.
segments.s161-g266: سپردار is the subject 'the commander'. با introduces the instrument 'with' (با تیر و ترکش = with arrows and (from) his quiver). ترکش is written as ترک + ش (possessive -sh = 'his quiver'); بدند again is the past/plural verb 'they struck/they were (doing so)'. The clause says the commander used arrows (from his quiver) as his weapon.
segments.s161-g267: نپرد is the negative of پریدن ('did not fly/soar'); here it is 3rd-person negative form. The phrase به کردار ایشان = 'in their manner' (به + کردار + pronoun ایشان). Note the subject عقاب comes at the end (verb then modifiers then subject) — a common poetic inversion: 'The eagle did not fly like them.'
segments.s161-g268: یکی را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker ('someone' with emphasis). سر اندر نیاید is a fixed idea 'sleep does not enter the head' (اندر = 'into/within', نیاید = 'does not come/appear'); بخواب is the noun/verb 'sleep' (here part of the idiom meaning 'sleep come upon'). Together: 'Sleep did not come upon anyone's head' → 'no one slept.'
segments.s161-g269: سه روز و سه شب is a temporal phrase 'three days and three nights'. بود is the past copula 'was/lasted'. هم is a linking/emphatic particle 'also/indeed' and زین نشان here means 'from/with this sign' or 'this sign' (zīn = 'of/from this' in poetic contraction). The clause says the sign persisted for that duration.
segments.s161-g270: غمی is the noun 'grief/sorrow' (here accusative/experiencer). شد is the verb 'became/occurred' (i.e. 'sorrow befell'). سر و اسپ 'heads and horses' are the things affected, and گردنکشان is a descriptive/epithet 'neck‑breakers' (i.e. proud rebels); the phrase means 'sorrow came upon the heads and steeds of the rebels.'
segments.s161-g271: ازیشان is a contracted prepositional phrase (از ایشان = 'from/among them'). کسی را is 'one/ someone' marked by را (definite/emphatic object/topic). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. خواب آمدی is the idiom 'sleep came (to him)' = 'he fell asleep' or 'sleep seized him' (آمدی here is the verbal element 'came' used impersonally with خواب).
segments.s161-g272: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form). جنگش = جنگ + ش 'his fighting/battle'. بدانگه is an adverbial 'to that place/thereupon' (بد + آن گاه). شتاب آمدی combines شتاب 'haste' with آمدی 'came' to mean 'he came hastily' or 'he hastened' — so the clause says he left the fight and hurried to that spot.
segments.s161-g281: بفرمود is an honorific/past-third-person verb meaning 'he ordered/said.' کز introduces a content clause ('that') and leads into the object of the order. نامداران هزار is a poetic word order for 'a thousand of the renowned' (نامداران = 'the famed/renowned ones'; هزار is the numeral placed after the noun by inversion).
segments.s161-g282: This is a compound of two imperatival/jussive actions commanded in the prior clause. بخوانید here functions as a commanded action 'call/summon' (or 'have them brought'). وز is a small poetic connective/particle in this construction (not the literal 'gust' or 'weight' — it functions like a linking 'and/for' and has no matching sense in the provided list). بزم سازید کار is 'prepare/arrange the banquet' where بزم = 'banquet/feast', سازید is the verbal imperative/subjunctive 'make/arrange/compose', and کار = 'affair/work/arrangement.'
segments.s161-g283: سراسر همه دشت is a fronted adverbial: 'all across the plain' (سراسر = 'throughout', همه reinforcing 'all'). پرچین نهید is the verbal phrase 'they placed/set up hedges'; نهید here is the verb form 'to place/set' used in the narrative as a commanded or reported action (poetic/older verb form).
segments.s161-g284: به سغد اندر is a locative phrase 'into/at Sogdiana' (به ... اندر = 'into/within'). آرایش چین نهید describes what was set there: 'they placed Chinese-style adornments' — آرایش = 'adornment/array', چین here names 'China/Chinese (style)' used adjectivally, and نهید again is the verbal form 'placed/set'.
segments.s161-g285: بدین سان is the fixed adverbial phrase 'in this way/thus' (here tokenized into parts; ب is the prefix). به شادی = 'with joy/in rejoicing.' گذر کرد روز is a poetic inversion where the noun گذر ('passing') combines with the light verb کرد ('did') to mean 'the day passed'; روز is the logical subject placed after the verbal phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s161-g286: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از چشم شد دور = 'became distant from the eye/sight' (از = 'from', چشم here used as 'sight', شد = 'became', دور = 'distant'). گیتی فروز follows as the subject or epithet applied to what became distant: گیتی فروز literally 'world-bright' (an epithet for the light of the world / daylight), i.e., the brilliant world-light receded from view.
segments.s161-g287: به خواب و به آرامش آمد شتاب is an action sequence where آمد ('came') takes adverbial qualifiers: به خواب و به آرامش = 'to sleep and to repose,' and شتاب ('haste') describes the manner — 'he came hastily' or 'he hastened to sleep and to rest.' The verb آمد is simple past 'came' and شتاب functions adverbially.
segments.s161-g288: بغلتید is a past-form verb meaning 'rolled/tumbled' (or 'threw himself/embraced' depending on context); here it denotes rolling over in sleep. بر جامه افراسیاب = 'upon Afrasiab's garment/robe' (بر = 'on/upon', جامه = 'garment', افراسیاب is the proper name). The clause describes the physical motion ending the line.
segments.s161-g273: بخفتی is a past (perfective) form meaning ‘(he) slept’ with the old -ی ending; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking two clauses; آسوده functions adverbially/adjectivally before the verb to mean 'at ease/relieved'; برخاستی is the past of برخاستن 'to rise/get up' (the same old perfective form). The order (adverb/adjective before verb) and the paired verbs give two successive past actions.
segments.s161-g274: به نوی is an adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning 'in a new manner' (به + نوی), placed before the noun phrase; یکی here is the indefinite numeral 'one/a certain' modifying جنگ 'battle/fight'; آراستی is the past (2/3rd-person) of آراییدن/آرایستن meaning 'to array/prepare/arrange'. The object phrase (به نوی یکی جنگ) precedes the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s161-g275: برآشفت is a past/past-result verbal form meaning 'he became enraged/grew agitated' (prefix بر- plus آشفتن). چون here is a simile conjunction 'like/as', and آتش is the metaphorical noun 'fire'; the proper name افراسیاب follows, giving an inverted order: 'like fire, Afrasiab became enraged.'
segments.s161-g276: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'); چندش is a noun meaning 'disgust/repugnance' (his aversion); چه is an interrogative/exclamative 'what/why/how'; گویی here is a noun meaning 'saying/talk' (i.e. 'what talk/saying'); ز is the poetic preposition 'about/from concerning'; آرام 'calm/rest' and خواب 'sleep' form the object of that preposition. Together the clause asks rhetorically why one would speak of rest and sleep given his fury.
segments.s161-g277: به marks the target of the gaze ('at/toward'); گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz'; اندر is a locative preposition 'in/within' adding a spatial nuance; چنان is an adverb/pronoun 'so/such'; بنگرید is the classical past/prefixed form of نگرن/نگریدن 'to look/gaze' (here 'he looked in such a way'). Word order places the object phrase before the verbal phrase.
segments.s161-g278: که introduces the result/consequence clause 'so that/that'; گفتی is used as the noun 'conversation/talk' (the phrasing 'گفتی میانش' = 'in their talk'); میانش is میان + enclitic -ش 'between them/among them'; بخواهد is the present/future-like 3rd-person form of خواستن meaning 'he wishes/will/means to'; برید here stands as the verb 'to cut/sever' (classical form; grammatically it is the verb governed by بخواهد: 'he meant to cut').
segments.s161-g279: یکی is the indefinite 'someone/a certain one' introducing an actor; بانگ (a shout/cry) is the noun object of the light verb construction بانگ برزد, where برزد is an archaic verbal form used with بانگ to mean 'a cry came/fell forth' or 'he uttered a cry'; براندش is the past form meaning 'he drove him away' (براند + enclitic -ش 'him'); ز پیش is a prepositional phrase 'from before/in front.'
segments.s161-g280: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; خواست is the past of خواستن 'he wanted/intended'; راندن is the infinitive 'to drive/expel' (governed by خواست); برو is an imperative/interjection 'go!/be off' used as an exclamation in speech; خشم خویش is a possessive construction: خشم 'anger' + خویش (خوی + enclitic -ش) 'his own' meaning 'his own anger' — here commenting that the action stems from his anger.
segments.s161-g129: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from'. دیده (dideh) 'eye' is the place from which something comes. همی (hamī) is the poetic continuous/iterative particle meaning 'kept/continually' and modifies the verbal action. خون فرو ریختند is the verb phrase: خون (blood) precedes the verb (typical Persian order) and فرو (foru) is an adverbial prefix 'down(ward)' attached before ریختند (past, 3pl of ریختن 'to pour/flow'), so the literal order is 'from the eye blood downward poured'.
segments.s161-g130: به (be) here functions adverbially/instrumentally 'by/with'. زاری (zarī) is the noun 'lamentation' and خروشی (kharūshī) 'a cry/clamor' is the event raised. برانگیختند (barāngīkhtand) is past 3rd plural of برانگیختن 'to arouse/raise', so the clause means 'they raised a cry with lamentation' (the preposition + noun phrase marks the manner).
segments.s161-g131: گواهی (gavāhī) 'testimony/evidence' is the direct object of دادن 'to give'; همی (hamī) again marks continuous action. داد (dād) is the past 'gave'. دل (del) is the logical subject whose state is being reported, and در شدن (dar shodan) is a nominalized verbal phrase 'to be split/broken' (در + شدن) describing the heart's condition; the whole sequence literally reads 'the heart kept giving testimony of becoming split'.
segments.s161-g132: که (ke) introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. دیدار (dīdār) 'meeting/seeing' names the hoped-for encounter. ازان پس (azān pas) = 'from that time on / thereafter'. نخواهد (nakhāhad) here functions as the future auxiliary 'will not' (the negative future meaning). بدن (badan) literally 'body' is used here figuratively: 'will have no body / will not remain alive' — i.e. 'will not live to see (again)'. The subject (the heart/person) is carried over from the previous clause and so is omitted here.
segments.s161-g133: چنین است (chenīn ast) is the fixed copular phrase 'thus is' or 'such is'. کردار (kardār) 'deed/conduct' is the noun being predicated, and گردنده دهر (gardande-ye dahr) is a genitive-like phrase where گردنده (present participle 'turning/revolving') modifies دهر (dahr, archaic 'time/age/world'). Together: 'Thus is the conduct of the turning world/time.'
segments.s161-g134: گهی (gahi) at the start means 'sometimes'. نوش (nūsh) is a poetic noun 'delight/drink' and بار (bār) 'load' combines as 'a load of delight'; آورد (āvard) is the past/simple present sense 'brings/carries'. گاه (gāh) introduces the contrasted time 'at times' and زهر (zahr) 'poison' completes the antithesis. The verb آورد is understood to apply to both nouns (parallelism typical in poetry).
segments.s161-g135: سوی (suy) is the preposition 'toward'. گاه (gāh) here is 'occasion/place/time' (part of a temporal/directional phrase). بنهاد (benahād) is past of نهادن 'to set/put' and with روی (rūy) 'face' yields the idiom 'بنهاد روی' 'turned/put (his) face (toward)'. Word order is inverted from neutral prose (the verb appears before the subject کاووس in the line), so the clause means 'Kaavus turned his face toward (that direction/occasion)'.
segments.s161-g136: سیاوش (Siyāvaš) is the subject. ابا (abā) is the classical verb/adjective 'to refuse, be unwilling' here used as 'refused / was unwilling'. لشکر (laškar) 'army' takes the modifier جنگ‌جوی (jangjū, 'warrior/war‑like') to form 'the warlike army' (a compound noun). The short clause reads 'Siyavash refused the warlike host' or 'Siyavash, unwilling, would not accept the army.'
